preview.en: A single decree from the Jade Emperor turns Heaven’s order into a crisis—and forces a young scribe to carry a message that even gods fear.
preview.id: Satu maklumat Kaisar Giok mengguncang ketertiban Langit—dan memaksa juru tulis muda membawa pesan yang bahkan para dewa takuti.

content.en:
Heaven ran on ritual the way rivers ran on gravity. Every bell had an hour, every star had a route, every wind had a name and a law that kept it from becoming a storm. In the Celestial Bureaucracy, nothing was ‘free’; even mercy required paperwork.

On the day the story begins, the clouds over the Hall of Radiant Administration did not drift as they should. They stacked like sealed envelopes. Lanterns hung too still. The air smelled faintly of ink—an omen that some decision had been written that could not be unwritten.

A junior scribe named Lin Yue arrived before dawn, as he always did. Lin Yue was the kind of immortal who had never held a sword, never commanded thunder, never been praised in temple songs. His gift was smaller and therefore more dangerous: he could remember exactly what was said, exactly as it was said.

He took his seat among rows of desks carved from pale jade and old cedar. Around him, other scribes sharpened their brushes, prepared seals, and whispered small gossip that never dared rise to conspiracy. No one conspired in Heaven. But everyone feared the moment when Heaven itself began to sound like a conspiracy.

A gong rang—once, then twice. The sound did not echo; it pressed down. The gates of the inner hall opened, and the minister of decrees entered carrying a scroll bound with gold thread.

Behind the minister, two celestial guards walked with the posture of men escorting a prisoner, except there was no prisoner. The prisoner was the scroll.

All scribes stood. Lin Yue stood too, but his eyes caught one detail: the gold thread around the scroll was tied in a knot used only for judgments that could not be appealed.

The minister did not unroll it at first. He knelt, placed the scroll upon a stand, and spoke the formal preface: that these words came from the Jade Emperor’s own hand and bore the weight of the Mandate.

Then the doors at the far end opened without announcement.

The Jade Emperor entered.

He did not arrive with thunder. Thunder was for lesser drama. He arrived with a quiet that made the court forget how to breathe. His robes held constellations like embroidery, and his crown looked less like jewelry than like a rule.

Lin Yue had seen the Emperor only once from a distance. Today the Emperor’s gaze swept the hall as if counting every soul present, as if Heaven had become something that needed auditing.

The Emperor took his seat and looked at the scroll. For a long moment he did nothing. That stillness was the first warning.

When he finally spoke, his voice was measured, almost gentle. He said Heaven was out of balance.

The southern realms were burning with unseasonal heat. The northern rivers were freezing too early. Mortal kings were praying harder, but their prayers were being answered with noise instead of clarity. The Emperor said these were not accidents. They were symptoms.

Then he named the cause: somewhere in the chain of duty—between star and season, oath and outcome—a principle had been broken.

Heaven’s law had been obeyed, yet the world was sick.

That meant the law itself might be incomplete.

The court went cold. A law that is wrong is worse than chaos, because it makes the wrong feel righteous.

The Jade Emperor declared a decree: a review of the Mandate would begin immediately. The Celestial Bureaucracy would search for the missing clause—the hidden debt unpaid, the neglected promise, the unrecorded kindness, the ignored warning.

To do so, Heaven would require testimony.

Not from gods.

From mortals.

A murmur rippled through the scribes, quickly smothered by discipline. Mortals were not supposed to testify against Heaven. Mortals were supposed to be grateful, not informative.

The Emperor raised a hand and the murmur died.

He explained the risk: if Heaven admitted error, faith could shatter. If Heaven refused to admit error, the world would shatter.

Then he announced the first assignment.

A messenger must descend to the mortal world with an edict sealed by the Emperor himself. The edict would not command worship or obedience. It would command truth.

The messenger would be forbidden from revealing heavenly wonders. No miracles. No demonstrations. Only a question delivered to the right people in the right places: ‘Where did the Mandate fail you?’

The minister of decrees looked at the rows of scribes and began to read names.

Lin Yue expected the name of a war-god, a storm-general, someone who could survive bandits and kings. Instead the minister read Lin Yue’s name.

The hall did not gasp—Heaven did not gasp—but Lin Yue felt the attention shift like a blade.

Lin Yue stepped forward, hands shaking slightly, and knelt. The minister placed the scroll in his arms. The gold-thread knot sat against his palms like a weight.

Lin Yue dared to speak one sentence, careful not to sound like refusal: ‘Why me?’

The Jade Emperor answered without anger. ‘Because you will not change the words.’

That was not praise. It was a warning.

The Emperor continued: the edict must reach three mortals—an honest judge, a disgraced monk, and a woman who had never once prayed yet never once lied. These three would answer Heaven differently. Their answers would not agree. Truth rarely did.

Lin Yue’s mouth went dry. The assignment was not to enforce. It was to listen.

Listening was harder than fighting.

The Emperor dismissed the hall. But before Lin Yue could stand, the Emperor added a final clause—spoken softly, meant only for Lin Yue.

‘You will be tested,’ he said. ‘Not by demons. By your need to be certain.’

Lin Yue bowed until his forehead touched the cold floor.

When he rose, the scroll felt heavier than jade.

He walked out of the hall into a corridor where clouds moved like paper sliding into files. A gate waited at the end, the kind that opened downward.

He looked back once. Heaven was still beautiful. That was the most frightening part.

Lin Yue stepped through the gate and began his descent, carrying a decree that could change the meaning of Heaven itself—and knowing that if he delivered it wrongly, the world would not merely suffer.

It would make sense while suffering.

And that, he understood, would be the true catastrophe.
content.id:
Langit berjalan dengan ritual seperti sungai berjalan dengan gravitasi. Setiap lonceng punya jamnya, setiap bintang punya lintasannya, setiap angin punya nama dan hukum yang menahannya agar tak berubah jadi badai. Di Birokrasi Langit, tak ada yang ‘gratis’; bahkan belas kasih butuh berkas.

Pada hari kisah ini dimulai, awan di atas Aula Administrasi Bercahaya tidak mengalir seperti biasanya. Mereka menumpuk seperti amplop-amplop tersegel. Lentera menggantung terlalu diam. Udara berbau samar tinta—pertanda ada keputusan yang sudah ditulis dan tak bisa ditarik kembali.

Seorang juru tulis yunior bernama Lin Yue datang sebelum fajar, seperti biasa. Lin Yue bukan abadi yang memegang pedang, bukan komandan petir, bukan nama yang dipuja dalam nyanyian kuil. Bakatnya lebih kecil dan karena itu lebih berbahaya: ia bisa mengingat persis apa yang diucapkan, persis sebagaimana diucapkan.

Ia duduk di antara barisan meja yang dipahat dari giok pucat dan kayu aras tua. Di sekelilingnya, para juru tulis lain menajamkan kuas, menyiapkan segel, dan berbisik gosip kecil yang tak pernah berani naik jadi konspirasi. Tak ada yang berkonspirasi di Langit. Tetapi semua orang takut pada momen ketika Langit sendiri mulai terdengar seperti konspirasi.

Sebuah gong berbunyi—sekali, lalu dua kali. Bunyi itu tidak bergema; ia menekan. Pintu aula dalam terbuka, dan menteri maklumat masuk membawa gulungan yang diikat benang emas.

Di belakang menteri, dua pengawal surgawi berjalan dengan sikap orang mengawal tahanan—kecuali tak ada tahanan. Tahanannya adalah gulungan itu.

Semua juru tulis berdiri. Lin Yue berdiri juga, tetapi matanya menangkap satu detail: benang emas itu diikat dengan simpul yang hanya dipakai untuk vonis yang tak bisa banding.

Menteri tidak langsung membuka gulungan. Ia berlutut, meletakkan gulungan pada penyangga, dan mengucapkan pembuka resmi: bahwa kata-kata ini datang dari tangan Kaisar Giok sendiri dan memikul berat Mandat.

Lalu pintu di ujung jauh terbuka tanpa pengumuman.

Kaisar Giok masuk.

Ia tidak datang dengan guntur. Guntur untuk drama tingkat bawah. Ia datang dengan keheningan yang membuat aula lupa cara bernapas. Jubahnya menyimpan rasi bintang seperti sulaman, dan mahkotanya tampak bukan perhiasan, melainkan aturan.

Lin Yue pernah melihat Kaisar sekali dari jauh. Hari ini, tatapan Kaisar menyapu aula seakan menghitung setiap jiwa yang hadir, seakan Langit sedang butuh audit.

Kaisar duduk dan menatap gulungan itu. Lama ia tak melakukan apa-apa. Keheningan itu adalah peringatan pertama.

Saat akhirnya bicara, suaranya terukur, nyaris lembut. Ia berkata Langit tidak seimbang.

Wilayah selatan terbakar oleh panas di luar musim. Sungai-sungai utara membeku terlalu dini. Raja-raja fana berdoa lebih keras, tetapi doa mereka dijawab dengan kebisingan, bukan kejernihan. Kaisar berkata itu bukan kebetulan. Itu gejala.

Lalu ia menyebut penyebab: di suatu titik dalam rantai tugas—antara bintang dan musim, sumpah dan akibat—sebuah prinsip telah patah.

Hukum Langit telah dipatuhi, namun dunia sakit.

Artinya hukum itu sendiri mungkin belum lengkap.

Aula mendadak dingin. Hukum yang salah lebih buruk daripada chaos, karena ia membuat yang salah terasa benar.

Kaisar Giok mengumumkan maklumat: peninjauan Mandat dimulai segera. Birokrasi Langit akan mencari ‘pasal yang hilang’—utang tersembunyi yang belum dibayar, janji yang diabaikan, kebaikan yang tak dicatat, peringatan yang tak digubris.

Untuk itu, Langit membutuhkan kesaksian.

Bukan dari para dewa.

Dari manusia fana.

Bisik-bisik kecil merambat cepat lalu mati oleh disiplin. Manusia tidak semestinya memberi kesaksian terhadap Langit. Manusia semestinya bersyukur, bukan memberi informasi.

Kaisar mengangkat tangan, dan bisik-bisik mati.

Ia menjelaskan risikonya: jika Langit mengakui kesalahan, iman bisa pecah. Jika Langit menolak mengakui kesalahan, dunia yang pecah.

Lalu ia mengumumkan tugas pertama.

Seorang utusan harus turun ke dunia fana membawa maklumat yang disegel langsung oleh Kaisar. Maklumat itu tidak memerintah penyembahan atau ketaatan. Ia memerintah kejujuran.

Utusan itu dilarang memperlihatkan keajaiban Langit. Tak ada mukjizat. Tak ada demonstrasi. Hanya sebuah pertanyaan yang harus sampai ke orang yang tepat di tempat yang tepat: ‘Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?’

Menteri maklumat menatap barisan juru tulis dan mulai membacakan nama.

Lin Yue mengira yang dipanggil akan dewa perang, jenderal badai, seseorang yang bisa selamat dari bandit dan raja. Namun menteri menyebut nama Lin Yue.

Aula tidak terkejut—Langit tidak pernah ‘terkejut’—tetapi Lin Yue merasakan perhatian bergeser seperti bilah.

Lin Yue maju, tangan sedikit gemetar, lalu berlutut. Menteri meletakkan gulungan itu di lengannya. Simpul benang emas menekan telapak tangannya seperti beban.

Lin Yue berani mengucapkan satu kalimat, hati-hati agar tak terdengar menolak: ‘Kenapa aku?’

Kaisar Giok menjawab tanpa marah. ‘Karena kau tidak akan mengubah kata-katanya.’

Itu bukan pujian. Itu peringatan.

Kaisar melanjutkan: maklumat harus mencapai tiga manusia—seorang hakim jujur, seorang biksu yang dipermalukan, dan seorang perempuan yang tidak pernah sekalipun berdoa namun tidak pernah sekalipun berbohong. Tiga orang ini akan menjawab Langit dengan cara berbeda. Jawaban mereka tidak akan sepakat. Kebenaran jarang sepakat.

Mulut Lin Yue kering. Tugasnya bukan menegakkan. Tugasnya mendengar.

Mendengar lebih sulit daripada bertarung.

Kaisar membubarkan aula. Namun sebelum Lin Yue berdiri, Kaisar menambahkan satu klausul terakhir—diucapkan pelan, hanya untuk Lin Yue.

‘Kau akan diuji,’ katanya. ‘Bukan oleh iblis. Oleh kebutuhanmu untuk merasa pasti.’

Lin Yue membungkuk sampai dahinya menyentuh lantai dingin.

Saat ia berdiri, gulungan itu terasa lebih berat dari giok.

Ia berjalan keluar melewati koridor tempat awan bergerak seperti kertas yang diselipkan ke berkas. Sebuah gerbang menunggu di ujung, gerbang yang terbuka ke bawah.

Lin Yue menoleh sekali. Langit tetap indah. Itu bagian yang paling menakutkan.

Lin Yue melangkah melewati gerbang dan memulai turunnya, membawa maklumat yang bisa mengubah makna Langit itu sendiri—dan tahu bahwa jika ia menyampaikannya salah, dunia tidak hanya akan menderita.

Dunia akan ‘masuk akal’ saat menderita.

Dan itulah, ia mengerti, bencana yang sesungguhnya.

content.en:
Lin Yue accepts the scroll with both hands, feeling the gold-thread knot bite into his palms like a silent warning. He turns to leave, rehearsing the Emperor’s words in his mind so he cannot misquote them.

Then the knot twitches.

At first he thinks it is his pulse. But the gold thread shifts again, as if the seal is breathing.

Lin Yue freezes. A sealed decree is not supposed to move. Heaven’s authority is meant to be still—absolute, finished. Motion implies debate.

He takes one careful step into the corridor and the seal tightens around the scroll, constricting as if it is trying to prevent itself from opening.

A drop of ink appears at the edge of the jade paper. Not spilled—born.

Lin Yue has never seen ink appear from nothing.

He looks back toward the hall. The guards have not moved. The minister is already leaving. The Emperor’s presence has withdrawn like the tide.

Lin Yue’s mind screams to report it. But his body remembers the Emperor’s warning: you will be tested by your need to be certain.

A cracking sound—soft, like ice breaking in a cup—comes from the knot. One strand loosens.

Lin Yue’s instincts split in two directions. The scribe in him wants to preserve the exact words. The living being in him wants to know what is trying to escape.

He chooses the rule he has lived by: do not change the words.

So he does not open the scroll.

Instead, he presses his thumb against the knot and whispers the formal oath of a courier: that the message will arrive intact, unaltered, and unadorned.

The oath is supposed to be ceremonial. But the corridor answers. The clouds around him shift, and the gold thread stiffens as if the oath has become a lock.

The ink drop stops growing.

Lin Yue exhales, but the relief is thin. He understands what almost happened: the decree wanted to rewrite itself.

He realizes a terrifying possibility. Heaven is not merely investigating an error. Heaven is infected by one.

As he walks toward the downward gate, Lin Yue feels the scroll grow colder in his arms, as if offended.

He is still the messenger. But now he is also the jailer of a message that might be alive.

Chapter 1 ends with Lin Yue stepping into the gate with a new fear: not that he will misdeliver the decree, but that the decree will try to deliver him.
content.id:
Lin Yue menerima gulungan itu dengan dua tangan, merasakan simpul benang emas menggigit telapak seperti peringatan tanpa suara. Ia berbalik untuk pergi, mengulang kata-kata Kaisar di pikirannya agar tak salah kutip.

Lalu simpul itu bergerak.

Mula-mula ia mengira itu detak nadinya. Namun benang emas bergeser lagi, seakan segel itu bernapas.

Lin Yue membeku. Maklumat tersegel tidak seharusnya bergerak. Otoritas Langit seharusnya diam—mutlak, selesai. Gerak berarti perdebatan.

Ia melangkah pelan ke koridor dan segel itu justru mengencang pada gulungan, mencekik seolah ingin mencegah dirinya sendiri terbuka.

Setitik tinta muncul di tepi kertas giok. Bukan tumpah—lahir.

Lin Yue belum pernah melihat tinta muncul dari ketiadaan.

Ia menoleh ke aula. Para pengawal tidak bergerak. Menteri sudah pergi. Kehadiran Kaisar surut seperti pasang.

Pikiran Lin Yue berteriak untuk melapor. Namun tubuhnya mengingat peringatan Kaisar: kau akan diuji oleh kebutuhanmu untuk merasa pasti.

Bunyi retak—lembut, seperti es pecah di gelas—datang dari simpul. Satu helai benang mengendur.

Naluri Lin Yue terbelah. Sang juru tulis ingin menjaga kata-kata tetap persis. Sang makhluk hidup ingin tahu apa yang mencoba lolos.

Ia memilih aturan yang ia hidupi: jangan ubah kata-katanya.

Maka ia tidak membuka gulungan.

Sebaliknya, ia menekan ibu jarinya pada simpul dan berbisik sumpah resmi kurir: pesan akan tiba utuh, tak diubah, tak dihias.

Sumpah itu seharusnya seremonial. Namun koridor menjawab. Awan di sekitarnya bergeser, dan benang emas mengeras seakan sumpah menjadi kunci.

Titik tinta berhenti membesar.

Lin Yue mengembuskan napas, tetapi lega itu tipis. Ia paham apa yang hampir terjadi: maklumat itu ingin menulis ulang dirinya.

Ia menyadari kemungkinan mengerikan: Langit bukan hanya menyelidiki kesalahan. Langit tertular oleh kesalahan itu.

Saat berjalan menuju gerbang yang terbuka ke bawah, Lin Yue merasakan gulungan itu makin dingin di lengannya, seolah tersinggung.

Ia masih utusan. Tetapi kini ia juga sipir bagi pesan yang mungkin hidup.

Chapter 1 berakhir ketika Lin Yue melangkah ke gerbang dengan ketakutan baru: bukan ia akan salah menyampaikan maklumat, melainkan maklumat itu akan mencoba menyampaikan dirinya.

content.en:
The Jade Emperor announces the decree in a voice so steady it makes the hall feel safe. Lin Yue listens, committing every syllable to memory the way a monk commits prayer.

The assignment is clear: descend, ask, listen, record.

Then, at the very end, the Emperor adds a clause that is not on the scroll.

Not a long speech. Just one word.

He repeats the edict’s question—'Where did the Mandate fail you?'—but the last time he speaks it, the word 'fail' changes into 'betray.'

Lin Yue’s breath catches.

A failure can be accidental. A betrayal is chosen.

Lin Yue glances at the minister holding the sealed scroll. The minister does not react. The other scribes do not react. It is as if the changed word never happened.

But Lin Yue heard it.

Because his curse and gift are the same: he remembers what was said, not what was meant.

When the hall dismisses, Lin Yue approaches the minister and asks to verify the text. The minister refuses, saying the Emperor’s spoken clarification is sufficient.

Lin Yue realizes the trap. Which version is canon—the written or the spoken? In Heaven, both are law. When they disagree, obedience becomes a knife.

Lin Yue is sent down with the written scroll. Yet the spoken word burns in his mind.

On the downward gate, an attendant spirit whispers a warning: 'If you ask mortals about betrayal, mortals will answer with blame. If you ask about failure, they might answer with truth.'

Lin Yue now carries two decrees: one on paper and one in memory.

He understands why he was chosen. Not because he will not change the words.

But because he cannot stop them from changing inside him.

Chapter 1 ends with Lin Yue stepping into the mortal world knowing that his first decision will come before he meets any mortal: which word will he deliver, and what kind of world that word will create.
content.id:
Kaisar Giok mengumumkan maklumat dengan suara yang begitu stabil sampai aula terasa aman. Lin Yue mendengarkan, mengukir setiap suku kata ke ingatan seperti biksu mengukir doa.

Tugasnya jelas: turun, bertanya, mendengar, mencatat.

Lalu, di bagian paling akhir, Kaisar menambahkan satu klausul yang tidak ada di gulungan.

Bukan pidato panjang. Hanya satu kata.

Kaisar mengulang pertanyaan maklumat—'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'—tetapi saat terakhir ia mengucapkannya, kata 'gagal' berubah menjadi 'mengkhianati.'

Napas Lin Yue tersangkut.

Gagal bisa tidak sengaja. Mengkhianati adalah pilihan.

Lin Yue melirik menteri yang memegang gulungan tersegel. Menteri tidak bereaksi. Para juru tulis lain tidak bereaksi. Seolah kata yang berubah itu tidak pernah terjadi.

Tetapi Lin Yue mendengarnya.

Karena kutuk dan berkahnya sama: ia mengingat apa yang diucapkan, bukan apa yang dimaksud.

Saat aula bubar, Lin Yue mendekati menteri dan meminta memverifikasi teks. Menteri menolak, berkata klarifikasi lisan Kaisar sudah cukup.

Lin Yue melihat jebakannya. Mana yang canon—tulisan atau ucapan? Di Langit, keduanya hukum. Saat keduanya bertentangan, ketaatan berubah jadi pisau.

Lin Yue tetap dikirim turun membawa gulungan tertulis. Namun kata lisan itu membakar di pikirannya.

Di gerbang turun, roh penjaga berbisik memperingatkan: 'Kalau kau bertanya manusia soal pengkhianatan, manusia akan menjawab dengan menyalahkan. Kalau kau bertanya soal kegagalan, mungkin mereka menjawab dengan kebenaran.'

Lin Yue kini memikul dua maklumat: satu di kertas, satu di ingatan.

Ia paham kenapa ia dipilih. Bukan karena ia tak akan mengubah kata-kata.

Melainkan karena ia tak bisa menghentikan kata-kata berubah di dalam dirinya.

Chapter 1 berakhir saat Lin Yue melangkah ke dunia fana sambil tahu keputusan pertamanya muncul sebelum ia bertemu manusia mana pun: kata mana yang akan ia sampaikan, dan dunia seperti apa yang akan diciptakan kata itu.

content.en:
Lin Yue hears the list: an honest judge, a disgraced monk, and a woman who never prays yet never lies. The hall accepts the instruction like routine.

But Lin Yue cannot.

He has lived in Heaven long enough to know that every list is incomplete. A list is a decision about who counts.

When the Emperor dismisses the court, Lin Yue remains kneeling longer than protocol allows. The minister of decrees signals him to rise. Lin Yue does not.

He asks a forbidden question: 'What if they refuse to speak?'

Silence falls. The scribes stare forward, pretending not to hear.

The Jade Emperor looks down at Lin Yue, and for the first time the Emperor’s gaze feels not administrative but personal.

The Emperor answers: 'Then Heaven must learn to listen in a new way.'

Lin Yue does not understand.

The Emperor continues: mortals refuse all the time. They refuse taxes, refuse kings, refuse love, refuse themselves. If Heaven cannot accept refusal, Heaven cannot govern humans—only punish them.

Lin Yue feels his throat tighten. He realizes the decree is not a command to mortals. It is a command to Heaven.

Then the Emperor adds the cruel twist: Lin Yue may not force testimony. He may not threaten, bribe, or reveal miracles. If the witnesses refuse, Lin Yue must return with refusal as the answer.

The hall is colder now. Refusal, recorded by a scribe, becomes evidence.

Lin Yue accepts the scroll and stands. He understands the danger: the mission can fail without any villain.

And if it fails, Heaven will have to decide whether it values truth more than obedience.

Chapter 1 ends not with Lin Yue’s descent, but with his realization: the hardest part of carrying a decree is carrying the possibility that it will not be fulfilled.
content.id:
Lin Yue mendengar daftar itu: hakim jujur, biksu yang dipermalukan, dan perempuan yang tak pernah berdoa namun tak pernah berbohong. Aula menerima instruksi itu seperti rutinitas.

Namun Lin Yue tidak.

Ia hidup di Langit cukup lama untuk tahu setiap daftar selalu tidak lengkap. Daftar adalah keputusan tentang siapa yang dianggap.

Saat Kaisar membubarkan sidang, Lin Yue tetap berlutut lebih lama dari yang diizinkan protokol. Menteri memberi isyarat agar ia berdiri. Lin Yue tidak.

Ia bertanya hal terlarang: 'Kalau mereka menolak bicara bagaimana?'

Sunyi jatuh. Para juru tulis menatap lurus, pura-pura tidak mendengar.

Kaisar Giok menatap Lin Yue, dan untuk pertama kalinya tatapan itu terasa bukan administratif, melainkan personal.

Kaisar menjawab: 'Kalau begitu Langit harus belajar mendengar dengan cara baru.'

Lin Yue tidak paham.

Kaisar melanjutkan: manusia sering menolak. Menolak pajak, menolak raja, menolak cinta, menolak diri sendiri. Kalau Langit tidak bisa menerima penolakan, Langit tidak bisa memerintah manusia—hanya menghukum.

Tenggorokan Lin Yue menegang. Ia sadar maklumat ini bukan perintah untuk manusia. Ini perintah untuk Langit.

Lalu Kaisar menambahkan twist yang kejam: Lin Yue tidak boleh memaksa kesaksian. Tidak boleh mengancam, menyuap, atau memperlihatkan mukjizat. Jika para saksi menolak, Lin Yue harus pulang membawa penolakan sebagai jawaban.

Aula menjadi lebih dingin. Penolakan, yang dicatat juru tulis, berubah menjadi bukti.

Lin Yue menerima gulungan itu dan berdiri. Ia mengerti bahayanya: misi ini bisa gagal tanpa penjahat.

Dan jika gagal, Langit harus memutuskan apakah ia lebih menghargai kebenaran atau ketaatan.

Chapter 1 berakhir bukan pada turunnya Lin Yue, melainkan pada kesadarannya: bagian tersulit membawa maklumat adalah membawa kemungkinan bahwa maklumat itu tidak akan terpenuhi.

content.en:
Lin Yue takes the scroll and walks toward the downward gate. Each step feels like a signature.

He reaches the corridor where clouds move like paperwork, and suddenly the beauty of Heaven feels like a trap: polished floors, perfect order, and no place to hide a mistake.

A thought forms—small, shameful, vivid: If he never delivers the decree, the decree cannot break anything.

He stops.

No guard stops him. Heaven rarely needs chains. It relies on identity.

Lin Yue turns the other way and walks—not toward the gate, but toward the archives.

If the Mandate is missing a clause, perhaps the clause is already here. Perhaps it was filed wrong centuries ago. Perhaps all of this could be solved without dragging mortals into Heaven’s guilt.

He slips between shelves that hold weather records, star routes, birth ledgers, and sealed calamities. Each shelf smells like a different kind of power.

Lin Yue hides the scroll inside an empty drawer and begins to search.

At first he feels relief. He is doing something useful. He is preventing panic.

Then the archives begin to react.

Files rearrange themselves. Ink crawls between margins. Seals loosen as if the room has decided that secrets are outdated.

Lin Yue realizes the archives are not passive. They are a memory-organ. And he has introduced a foreign command into memory.

The drawer rattles. The scroll wants to be carried.

Lin Yue backs away, terrified. He hears footsteps—silent, measured.

An inspector spirit appears, faceless and polite. It asks Lin Yue for his delivery receipt.

Lin Yue tries to lie. The lie fails before it leaves his mouth.

The inspector does not punish him. It simply writes down the fact of his refusal.

That is the punishment.

Because once refusal is recorded in Heaven, it becomes precedent.

Lin Yue understands too late: he did not stop the decree.

He created a new clause in the Mandate—one that says even Heaven can disobey itself.

Chapter 1 ends with Lin Yue being escorted not to a dungeon, but to a desk. He is ordered to write his own act into law, and he learns the true horror of bureaucracy: mistakes do not vanish.

They become policy.
content.id:
Lin Yue membawa gulungan itu dan berjalan menuju gerbang yang terbuka ke bawah. Setiap langkah terasa seperti tanda tangan.

Ia tiba di koridor tempat awan bergerak seperti berkas, dan tiba-tiba keindahan Langit terasa seperti jebakan: lantai mengilap, keteraturan sempurna, dan tak ada tempat menyembunyikan kesalahan.

Sebuah pikiran terbentuk—kecil, memalukan, namun jelas: Kalau ia tak pernah menyampaikan maklumat itu, maklumat itu tak bisa merusak apa pun.

Ia berhenti.

Tak ada penjaga yang menghentikannya. Langit jarang butuh rantai. Langit mengandalkan identitas.

Lin Yue berbalik dan berjalan—bukan menuju gerbang, melainkan menuju arsip.

Jika Mandat kekurangan pasal, mungkin pasal itu sudah ada di sini. Mungkin salah disimpan ratusan tahun lalu. Mungkin semuanya bisa diselesaikan tanpa menyeret manusia ke rasa bersalah Langit.

Ia menyelinap di antara rak-rak yang menyimpan catatan cuaca, rute bintang, daftar kelahiran, dan bencana yang tersegel. Setiap rak berbau jenis kuasa yang berbeda.

Lin Yue menyembunyikan gulungan itu di laci kosong dan mulai mencari.

Mula-mula ia lega. Ia merasa berguna. Ia mencegah kepanikan.

Lalu arsip bereaksi.

Berkas-berkas menyusun ulang diri. Tinta merayap di antara margin. Segel-segel mengendur seakan ruangan memutuskan rahasia sudah kedaluwarsa.

Lin Yue sadar arsip bukan benda mati. Ia organ ingatan. Dan ia baru saja memasukkan perintah asing ke dalam ingatan.

Laci bergetar. Gulungan itu ingin dibawa.

Lin Yue mundur, ketakutan. Ia mendengar langkah kaki—sunyi, terukur.

Seorang roh inspektur muncul, tanpa wajah namun sopan. Ia meminta tanda terima pengiriman.

Lin Yue mencoba berbohong. Bohong itu gagal sebelum keluar dari mulut.

Inspektur itu tidak menghukumnya. Ia hanya menuliskan fakta penolakan Lin Yue.

Itulah hukumannya.

Karena begitu penolakan tercatat di Langit, ia menjadi preseden.

Lin Yue terlambat paham: ia tidak menghentikan maklumat.

Ia menciptakan pasal baru dalam Mandat—pasal yang berkata bahkan Langit bisa membangkang pada dirinya sendiri.

Chapter 1 berakhir ketika Lin Yue tidak diantar ke penjara, melainkan ke meja kerja. Ia diperintahkan menulis tindakannya sendiri menjadi hukum, dan ia belajar horor sejati birokrasi: kesalahan tidak menghilang.

Kesalahan menjadi kebijakan.

preview.en: Lin Yue descends into the mortal world under strict constraints, discovers how fragile truth becomes without miracles, and reaches the first witness: an honest judge.
preview.id: Lin Yue turun ke dunia fana dengan batasan ketat, menyadari rapuhnya kebenaran tanpa mukjizat, lalu tiba pada saksi pertama: seorang hakim yang jujur.

content.en:
The downward gate was not a door so much as a decision made physical. Lin Yue stood before it with the Emperor’s sealed scroll pressed to his chest, feeling the gold-thread knot like a second heartbeat.

A gatekeeper spirit waited beside the threshold, thin as a brushstroke and quiet as a margin note. It did not salute. It did not congratulate. It only verified.

Lin Yue was required to recite the courier’s constraints aloud—each one a nail driven into his mission.

First: he may not reveal heavenly wonders to compel belief.

Second: he may not threaten, bribe, or promise reward.

Third: he must deliver the Emperor’s words intact, and he must record the mortal answers without interpretation.

The gatekeeper listened and stamped an invisible seal into Lin Yue’s shadow. Lin Yue felt it like a cold ring closing around his ankles.

‘If you break a constraint,’ the gatekeeper said, ‘your words will still be heard. But they will not mean what you intend.’

Lin Yue wanted to ask what that meant. The gatekeeper did not invite questions.

The gate opened downward.

Stepping through was like stepping out of grammar. Heaven had rules that made everything legible. The mortal world was not illegible; it was contradictory on purpose. Lin Yue felt gravity seize him—not as a law, but as a hunger.

He fell through layers of cloud that did not behave like Heaven’s paperwork. These clouds smelled of smoke, wet soil, and human breath. They were messy, alive, and full of unfinished stories.

He landed in a mountain pass at the edge of a province that still believed in dragons the way it believed in weather: not always, but enough to fear.

Lin Yue’s robes had changed. The constellations were gone. His sleeves looked like plain traveling cloth. The scroll remained, but its glow was muted, like a lamp hidden under a bowl.

He understood the first cruelty of the mission: Heaven had stripped him of credibility. In the mortal world, truth did not arrive with an aura. It arrived with a person.

He walked down toward a town whose rooftops clustered like tired animals around a river. Bells rang, but the bells were imperfect; their metal carried dents and history.

At the town gate, a guard stopped him and asked his business. Lin Yue offered the simplest answer: he was a courier.

The guard asked: whose courier?

Lin Yue nearly said the truth. The invisible seal around his ankles tightened. He remembered the constraint. No miracles. No revelation that would force faith.

He said he carried an official question for the magistrate.

The guard laughed and told him everyone carried questions. Lin Yue felt the mortal world’s first defense: mockery. It was cheaper than skepticism and safer than belief.

Lin Yue waited, then offered a small detail that was not miraculous—only precise: the magistrate’s full title, the exact date of the next court session, the names of two clerks who would be present. These were public facts, but spoken with a certainty that sounded like authority.

The guard’s laughter weakened into caution. He let Lin Yue pass.

Lin Yue entered the courthouse the next morning. The building was not grand, but it was busy with human suffering in neat piles: petitions, disputes, accusations. Truth here was not an ideal; it was a commodity everyone wanted to buy at a discount.

He asked for Judge Ren.

People looked at him oddly, as if he had asked for a myth. Someone pointed him to a side room.

Judge Ren was not seated on a high chair. He sat at a low desk, sleeves rolled up, copying testimony in his own hand. His ink-stained fingers were a quiet insult to corruption.

He looked up at Lin Yue with tired eyes and said, ‘If you’re here to flatter me, you’re late. If you’re here to threaten me, you’re early.’

Lin Yue bowed and presented the sealed scroll without opening it. He announced, in the formal language of the Celestial Bureaucracy translated into mortal courtesy, that he carried one question authorized by a mandate beyond kings.

Judge Ren did not reach for the scroll. He studied Lin Yue’s face.

‘Beyond kings,’ he repeated, unimpressed. ‘Then why send a boy with no soldiers?’

Lin Yue felt shame and relief at once. The judge was honest enough to refuse spectacle.

Lin Yue answered carefully: because soldiers make people lie.

Judge Ren’s expression softened by a fraction.

Lin Yue delivered the question exactly: ‘Where did the Mandate fail you?’

For a long moment, Judge Ren said nothing. Then he laughed once—dry, without joy.

‘You want me to speak against Heaven?’ he asked.

Lin Yue replied: Heaven asked to be answered.

Judge Ren leaned back and tapped the desk, once per heartbeat, as if deciding whether he trusted silence more than speech.

At last he spoke, not as an accusation but as a ledger.

He said the Mandate failed in small places first: a drought relief fund that vanished in transit; a law written to protect farmers but enforced only against the poor; a flood gate repaired on paper but left broken in wood; a tax exemption sold to the wealthy as “piety.”

He said the Mandate failed because people learned to treat virtue as decoration. They learned to perform righteousness while moving harm through procedure.

Lin Yue wrote every word. He did not argue. He did not comfort. He listened.

Judge Ren stopped and looked at Lin Yue sharply.

‘If Heaven truly cares,’ he said, ‘it must learn the difference between order and justice. Order is easy. Justice costs someone something real.’

Lin Yue felt the sentence land like a stone inside him. In Heaven, cost was measured in paperwork. Here, cost was measured in hunger and shame.

Judge Ren pushed a small bundle across the desk: a stack of copied testimonies.

‘Take these,’ he said. ‘Not as proof. As weight. So your masters feel what their words did down here.’

Lin Yue hesitated. The constraints forbade bribes and gifts. Was this a gift?

Judge Ren answered without being asked. ‘It’s not for you. It’s for your record.’

Lin Yue accepted the bundle. The invisible seal around his ankles did not tighten. He took that as permission.

As Lin Yue left the courthouse, he realized Chapter 2’s truth: the mission was not to hunt a single cosmic error. It was to gather a thousand ordinary betrayals committed by people who still called themselves loyal.

He looked at the sealed scroll and understood the deeper danger. If Heaven revised the Mandate, it would not only judge mortals.

It would have to judge the machinery that makes mortals into liars.

Chapter 2 ends with Lin Yue stepping back into the street carrying not only the Emperor’s decree, but Judge Ren’s testimonies—paper heavy enough to make even Heaven’s hands tremble.
content.id:
Gerbang turun bukan pintu biasa, melainkan keputusan yang dibuat menjadi benda. Lin Yue berdiri di depannya dengan gulungan tersegel Kaisar menempel di dada, merasakan simpul benang emas seperti detak jantung kedua.

Seorang penjaga gerbang menunggu di sisi ambang, tipis seperti sapuan kuas dan senyap seperti catatan pinggir. Ia tidak memberi hormat. Ia tidak memberi selamat. Ia hanya memverifikasi.

Lin Yue diwajibkan mengucapkan batasan kurir dengan suara jelas—satu per satu, seperti paku yang dipukul ke dalam misinya.

Pertama: ia tidak boleh memperlihatkan keajaiban Langit untuk memaksa orang percaya.

Kedua: ia tidak boleh mengancam, menyuap, atau menjanjikan hadiah.

Ketiga: ia harus menyampaikan kata-kata Kaisar utuh, dan ia harus mencatat jawaban manusia tanpa menafsirkannya.

Penjaga gerbang mendengarkan lalu menstempel segel tak kasatmata ke bayangan Lin Yue. Lin Yue merasakannya seperti cincin dingin yang mengikat pergelangan kakinya.

‘Kalau kau melanggar batasan,’ kata penjaga, ‘kata-katamu tetap akan didengar. Tapi maknanya tidak akan menjadi seperti yang kau maksud.’

Lin Yue ingin bertanya maksudnya apa. Penjaga tidak membuka ruang untuk pertanyaan.

Gerbang itu terbuka ke bawah.

Melangkah melewatinya seperti melangkah keluar dari tata bahasa. Langit punya aturan yang membuat segalanya terbaca. Dunia fana bukan tak terbaca; ia kontradiktif dengan sengaja. Lin Yue merasakan gravitasi menangkapnya—bukan sebagai hukum, melainkan sebagai lapar.

Ia jatuh menembus lapisan awan yang tidak berperilaku seperti berkas Langit. Awan ini berbau asap, tanah basah, dan napas manusia. Berantakan, hidup, dan penuh kisah yang belum selesai.

Ia mendarat di celah pegunungan di pinggir sebuah provinsi yang masih percaya naga seperti ia percaya cuaca: tidak selalu, tapi cukup untuk takut.

Jubah Lin Yue berubah. Rasi bintang hilang. Lengan bajunya tampak seperti kain pengelana biasa. Gulungan itu tetap ada, tetapi cahayanya redup, seperti lampu yang ditutup mangkuk.

Ia mengerti kekejaman pertama misi ini: Langit mencopot kredibilitasnya. Di dunia fana, kebenaran tidak datang bersama aura. Kebenaran datang bersama orang.

Ia berjalan turun menuju kota kecil yang atap-atapnya berkumpul seperti hewan lelah di sekitar sungai. Lonceng berdentang, tetapi dentangnya tidak sempurna; logamnya membawa penyok dan sejarah.

Di gerbang kota, seorang penjaga menghentikannya dan menanyakan urusannya. Lin Yue memberi jawaban paling sederhana: ia kurir.

Penjaga bertanya: kurir siapa?

Lin Yue hampir menyebut kebenaran. Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kakinya menegang. Ia ingat batasannya. Tanpa mukjizat. Tanpa pengungkapan yang memaksa iman.

Ia berkata ia membawa pertanyaan resmi untuk magistrat.

Penjaga tertawa dan bilang semua orang membawa pertanyaan. Lin Yue merasakan pertahanan pertama dunia fana: olok-olok. Lebih murah dari skeptis dan lebih aman dari percaya.

Lin Yue menunggu, lalu memberi detail kecil yang bukan mukjizat—hanya presisi: gelar lengkap magistrat, tanggal persis sidang berikutnya, nama dua juru tulis yang akan hadir. Fakta ini publik, tetapi diucapkan dengan kepastian yang terdengar seperti otoritas.

Tawa penjaga melemah menjadi hati-hati. Ia membiarkan Lin Yue masuk.

Keesokan paginya Lin Yue memasuki gedung pengadilan. Bangunannya tidak megah, tetapi sibuk oleh derita manusia dalam tumpukan rapi: petisi, sengketa, tuduhan. Kebenaran di sini bukan ideal; ia komoditas yang semua orang ingin beli dengan diskon.

Ia menanyakan Hakim Ren.

Orang-orang menatap aneh, seolah ia menyebut mitos. Seseorang menunjuk sebuah ruangan samping.

Hakim Ren tidak duduk di kursi tinggi. Ia duduk di meja rendah, lengan baju digulung, menyalin kesaksian dengan tangannya sendiri. Jari-jarinya yang berlumur tinta adalah hinaan sunyi bagi korupsi.

Ia menatap Lin Yue dengan mata lelah dan berkata, ‘Kalau kau datang untuk memuji, kau terlambat. Kalau kau datang untuk mengancam, kau terlalu cepat.’

Lin Yue membungkuk dan mengajukan gulungan tersegel tanpa membukanya. Ia mengumumkan, dalam bahasa resmi Birokrasi Langit yang diterjemahkan menjadi sopan santun fana, bahwa ia membawa satu pertanyaan yang disahkan mandat di atas raja.

Hakim Ren tidak meraih gulungan. Ia memeriksa wajah Lin Yue.

‘Di atas raja,’ ia mengulang, tak terkesan. ‘Kalau begitu kenapa mengirim bocah tanpa prajurit?’

Lin Yue merasakan malu sekaligus lega. Hakim ini jujur cukup untuk menolak tontonan.

Lin Yue menjawab hati-hati: karena prajurit membuat orang berbohong.

Raut Hakim Ren melunak sedikit.

Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan itu persis: ‘Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?’

Lama Hakim Ren diam. Lalu ia tertawa sekali—kering, tanpa senang.

‘Kau mau aku bicara melawan Langit?’ tanyanya.

Lin Yue menjawab: Langit meminta untuk dijawab.

Hakim Ren bersandar dan mengetuk meja, sekali per detak, seolah memutuskan apakah ia lebih percaya sunyi atau kata.

Akhirnya ia bicara, bukan sebagai tuduhan melainkan sebagai buku kas.

Ia berkata Mandat gagal pertama-tama di tempat kecil: dana bantuan kekeringan yang lenyap di perjalanan; hukum yang ditulis untuk melindungi petani tetapi hanya ditegakkan pada yang miskin; pintu air banjir yang diperbaiki di kertas namun dibiarkan rusak di kayu; pembebasan pajak yang dijual pada orang kaya sebagai ‘kesalehan’.

Ia berkata Mandat gagal karena orang belajar menjadikan kebajikan sebagai dekorasi. Mereka belajar menampilkan kebenaran sementara memindahkan luka lewat prosedur.

Lin Yue menulis tiap kata. Ia tidak membantah. Ia tidak menghibur. Ia mendengar.

Hakim Ren berhenti dan menatap Lin Yue tajam.

‘Kalau Langit benar peduli,’ katanya, ‘Langit harus belajar beda antara tertib dan adil. Tertib itu mudah. Adil itu membuat seseorang membayar sesuatu yang nyata.’

Kalimat itu jatuh di dalam Lin Yue seperti batu. Di Langit, biaya diukur berkas. Di sini, biaya diukur lapar dan malu.

Hakim Ren mendorong sebuah bungkusan kecil ke atas meja: tumpukan salinan kesaksian.

‘Bawa ini,’ katanya. ‘Bukan sebagai bukti. Sebagai beban. Biar para penguasa itu merasakan apa yang kata-kata mereka lakukan di sini.’

Lin Yue ragu. Batasan melarang suap dan hadiah. Apakah ini hadiah?

Hakim Ren menjawab tanpa ditanya. ‘Itu bukan buatmu. Itu buat catatanmu.’

Lin Yue menerima bungkusan itu. Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kakinya tidak mengencang. Ia menganggap itu izin.

Saat Lin Yue meninggalkan pengadilan, ia sadar kebenaran Chapter 2: tugasnya bukan memburu satu kesalahan kosmik. Tugasnya mengumpulkan seribu pengkhianatan biasa yang dilakukan orang-orang yang masih menyebut dirinya setia.

Ia menatap gulungan tersegel dan mengerti bahaya yang lebih dalam. Jika Langit merevisi Mandat, Langit bukan hanya mengadili manusia.

Langit harus mengadili mesin yang membuat manusia menjadi pembohong.

Chapter 2 berakhir ketika Lin Yue melangkah kembali ke jalan kota membawa bukan hanya maklumat Kaisar, tetapi juga kesaksian Hakim Ren—kertas yang cukup berat untuk membuat bahkan tangan Langit gemetar.

content.en:
Lin Yue recites the constraints with a steady voice, trying to make them sound like vows instead of chains. The gatekeeper spirit stamps the invisible seal into his shadow.

The seal feels cold around his ankles, but manageable—like a reminder, not a threat.

He steps through the downward gate and falls into the mortal world.

At first the mission seems possible. He can speak politely, ask for the judge, deliver the question, record the answer. He does not need miracles.

Then the first real obstacle appears: a clerk at the courthouse who smiles too widely.

The clerk offers Lin Yue tea and a chair. The clerk says Judge Ren is busy, and that official questions must go through official channels. He asks Lin Yue to write his question down.

Lin Yue hesitates. The decree is sealed. The Emperor’s words are not meant to be copied by mortal hands.

The clerk leans closer and lowers his voice: 'Or we can make you disappear quietly. Couriers get lost on the roads all the time.'

Lin Yue feels the seal tighten.

Not on his ankles—on his tongue.

A sudden dryness grips his mouth. The words he prepared become slippery. He can still speak, but each sentence arrives half a beat late, as if truth itself is being delayed.

He realizes the meaning of the gatekeeper’s warning: break a constraint, and your words will still be heard, but they will not mean what you intend.

The clerk is forcing him toward a violation. If Lin Yue threatens back, he breaks the rule. If he reveals Heaven, he breaks the rule. If he bribes, he breaks the rule.

And if he stays polite, he may never reach Judge Ren.

Lin Yue chooses a fourth path: he tells the clerk a truth that is not miraculous but is sharp enough to cut.

He says: 'If you stop a question from reaching an honest judge, you are declaring war on the idea of law. In war, even liars become evidence.'

The clerk laughs—then falters, because the sentence is not a threat. It is a diagnosis.

Lin Yue opens his satchel and shows the copied testimonies Judge Ren is known to keep. He does not reveal Heaven. He reveals the clerk’s fear: that someone is watching.

The clerk backs away. He suddenly becomes very respectful.

Lin Yue is led into Judge Ren’s room.

Judge Ren listens, and when Lin Yue finishes delivering the question, the judge says something different in this path: 'The Mandate failed because small men learned how to use big rules.'

Lin Yue records it.

As Lin Yue leaves, the seal around his ankles loosens for a moment—like an animal satisfied that he survived without cheating.

Chapter 2 ends with Lin Yue understanding the mortal world’s first lesson: miracles are not the only way to force truth. Sometimes the only miracle is refusing to be pushed into becoming the thing you came to investigate.
content.id:
Lin Yue mengucapkan batasan-batasan itu dengan suara stabil, berusaha membuatnya terdengar seperti sumpah, bukan rantai. Penjaga gerbang menstempel segel tak kasatmata ke bayangannya.

Segel itu terasa dingin di pergelangan kaki, tetapi masih bisa ditahan—seperti pengingat, bukan ancaman.

Ia melangkah melewati gerbang turun dan jatuh ke dunia fana.

Awalnya misi tampak mungkin. Ia bisa bicara sopan, meminta hakim, menyampaikan pertanyaan, mencatat jawaban. Ia tidak butuh mukjizat.

Lalu rintangan nyata pertama muncul: seorang juru tulis pengadilan yang senyumnya terlalu lebar.

Juru tulis itu menawarkan teh dan kursi. Ia berkata Hakim Ren sibuk, dan pertanyaan resmi harus lewat jalur resmi. Ia meminta Lin Yue menuliskan pertanyaannya.

Lin Yue ragu. Maklumat itu tersegel. Kata-kata Kaisar tidak dimaksudkan untuk disalin tangan fana.

Juru tulis itu mendekat dan merendahkan suara: 'Atau kami bisa membuatmu hilang pelan-pelan. Kurir sering tersesat di jalan.'

Lin Yue merasakan segel mengencang.

Bukan di pergelangan kaki—di lidahnya.

Mulutnya mendadak kering. Kata-kata yang ia siapkan menjadi licin. Ia masih bisa bicara, tetapi setiap kalimat datang terlambat setengah detak, seakan kebenaran sendiri sedang ditunda.

Ia paham maksud peringatan penjaga: jika melanggar batasan, kata-katamu tetap didengar, tapi maknanya tidak akan jadi seperti yang kau maksud.

Juru tulis itu sedang mendorongnya melanggar. Jika Lin Yue mengancam balik, ia melanggar. Jika ia membuka Langit, ia melanggar. Jika ia menyuap, ia melanggar.

Dan jika ia tetap sopan, ia mungkin tak akan pernah bertemu Hakim Ren.

Lin Yue memilih jalan keempat: ia mengatakan kebenaran yang bukan mukjizat, tetapi cukup tajam untuk memotong.

Ia berkata: 'Kalau kau menghalangi pertanyaan sampai ke hakim jujur, kau sedang menyatakan perang pada gagasan hukum. Dalam perang, bahkan pembohong menjadi bukti.'

Juru tulis itu tertawa—lalu ragu, karena kalimat itu bukan ancaman. Itu diagnosis.

Lin Yue membuka tasnya dan memperlihatkan salinan kesaksian yang dikenal disimpan Hakim Ren. Ia tidak membuka Langit. Ia membuka ketakutan si juru tulis: bahwa seseorang sedang melihat.

Juru tulis mundur. Mendadak ia jadi sangat sopan.

Lin Yue dibawa masuk ke ruangan Hakim Ren.

Hakim Ren mendengarkan, dan saat Lin Yue selesai menyampaikan pertanyaan, hakim itu berkata berbeda dalam jalur ini: 'Mandat gagal karena orang kecil belajar memakai aturan besar.'

Lin Yue mencatatnya.

Saat Lin Yue pergi, segel di pergelangan kakinya mengendur sesaat—seperti hewan yang puas karena ia selamat tanpa curang.

Chapter 2 berakhir dengan Lin Yue paham pelajaran pertama dunia fana: mukjizat bukan satu-satunya cara memaksa kebenaran. Kadang mukjizatnya adalah menolak didorong menjadi hal yang sedang kau selidiki.

content.en:
Lin Yue reaches the courthouse expecting to find Judge Ren like a lighthouse: a single steady point in a sea of human compromise.

He asks for the judge.

The clerks exchange glances that are too smooth.

They lead Lin Yue to a side room where a man sits at a desk with ink-stained fingers and tired eyes—the exact image Lin Yue was told to expect.

The man introduces himself as Judge Ren.

Lin Yue feels relief. He delivers the question.

The man answers immediately, fluently, almost beautifully. He speaks of drought funds stolen, laws twisted, virtue performed. He offers a perfect ledger of corruption.

Lin Yue writes fast, grateful.

Then the seal around his ankles tightens.

Not painful—warning.

Lin Yue pauses and asks a small verifying detail, a fact he knows is public: the name of the river gatekeeper responsible for last year’s flood repair.

The man hesitates.

Only a breath. But in Heaven, a breath is evidence.

The man gives a name—wrong.

Lin Yue’s stomach drops. The man is an imitation.

The courthouse has manufactured an 'honest judge' to control what Heaven hears.

Lin Yue cannot accuse him outright without breaking constraints. He cannot threaten. He cannot reveal Heaven. He cannot bribe.

So Lin Yue does the only thing left: he asks the decree’s question again, but changes the target.

He asks: 'Where did the Mandate fail you—right now, in this room?'

The imitation judge smiles wider and tells Lin Yue not to be clever.

Lin Yue bows and says one sentence with perfect calm: 'Then I will record that Heaven’s witness was replaced.'

The room goes still.

The imitation judge’s eyes sharpen. Clerks outside shift.

Lin Yue is escorted out politely, too politely.

On the street, a child runs into him and presses a folded note into his sleeve before sprinting away.

The note contains two words: 'Real. Basement.'

Lin Yue returns to the courthouse that night and finds a hidden stair.

In the damp basement, he finds Judge Ren—alive, bound, and gagged, ink on his fingers like a last stubborn proof.

Lin Yue frees him without calling it rescue, because rescue would make the mission about heroism.

Judge Ren speaks with a voice raw from silence: 'Now you understand. They don’t fear Heaven. They fear records.'

Chapter 2 ends with Lin Yue realizing the mortal world’s most sophisticated defense: it cannot silence truth, so it produces a counterfeit version that sounds true enough to be accepted.
content.id:
Lin Yue tiba di pengadilan berharap menemukan Hakim Ren seperti mercusuar: satu titik stabil di lautan kompromi manusia.

Ia meminta sang hakim.

Para juru tulis saling bertukar pandang yang terlalu mulus.

Mereka membawa Lin Yue ke ruangan samping, tempat seorang pria duduk dengan jari berlumur tinta dan mata lelah—citra persis yang Lin Yue diharapkan lihat.

Pria itu memperkenalkan diri sebagai Hakim Ren.

Lin Yue lega. Ia menyampaikan pertanyaan.

Pria itu menjawab segera, lancar, nyaris indah. Ia bicara soal dana kekeringan dicuri, hukum dipelintir, kebajikan dipamerkan. Ia memberi buku kas korupsi yang sempurna.

Lin Yue menulis cepat, bersyukur.

Lalu segel di pergelangan kakinya mengencang.

Bukan sakit—peringatan.

Lin Yue berhenti dan bertanya satu detail verifikasi kecil, fakta publik yang ia tahu: nama penjaga pintu air yang bertanggung jawab atas perbaikan banjir tahun lalu.

Pria itu ragu.

Hanya sehela napas. Tetapi di Langit, napas adalah bukti.

Pria itu menyebut nama—salah.

Perut Lin Yue jatuh. Pria itu tiruan.

Pengadilan telah membuat 'hakim jujur' palsu untuk mengendalikan apa yang akan didengar Langit.

Lin Yue tak bisa menuduh terang-terangan tanpa melanggar batasan. Ia tak boleh mengancam. Tak boleh membuka Langit. Tak boleh menyuap.

Jadi Lin Yue melakukan satu-satunya hal tersisa: ia mengajukan pertanyaan maklumat lagi, tetapi mengganti sasarannya.

Ia bertanya: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu—sekarang, di ruangan ini?'

Tiruan hakim itu tersenyum lebih lebar dan menyuruh Lin Yue berhenti pintar.

Lin Yue membungkuk dan berkata tenang: 'Kalau begitu aku akan mencatat bahwa saksi Langit diganti.'

Ruangan mendadak hening.

Mata tiruan itu menajam. Juru tulis di luar bergeser.

Lin Yue diantar keluar dengan sopan—terlalu sopan.

Di jalan, seorang anak menabraknya lalu menyelipkan catatan terlipat ke lengan bajunya sebelum lari.

Catatan itu berisi dua kata: 'Asli. Bawah.'

Malamnya Lin Yue kembali dan menemukan tangga tersembunyi.

Di ruang bawah tanah lembap, ia menemukan Hakim Ren—hidup, terikat, dan disumpal, tinta di jarinya seperti bukti terakhir yang keras kepala.

Lin Yue membebaskannya tanpa menyebutnya penyelamatan, karena penyelamatan akan mengubah misi menjadi soal heroisme.

Hakim Ren bicara dengan suara serak: 'Sekarang kau paham. Mereka tidak takut Langit. Mereka takut catatan.'

Chapter 2 berakhir dengan Lin Yue sadar pertahanan paling canggih dunia fana: mereka tak bisa membungkam kebenaran, jadi mereka memproduksi versi palsu yang terdengar cukup benar agar diterima.

content.en:
Judge Ren pushes the bundle of copied testimonies across the desk like a man pushing a weight he is tired of carrying.

Lin Yue accepts it and feels immediate relief: evidence. Something tangible to bring back to Heaven.

Outside, in the alley behind the courthouse, Lin Yue opens the bundle to check that the pages are in order.

The ink shifts.

Lines blur, then straighten into new sentences.

Lin Yue’s heart jumps. He flips a page. The names change. The dates change. The crimes become cleaner, smaller, almost reasonable.

The testimonies are rewriting themselves.

Lin Yue realizes the horror: corruption is not only in people. It is in records. In systems. In the very language that claims to describe truth.

He clamps the pages shut and presses his palm on them, trying to force stillness.

The invisible seal on his ankles tightens. The gatekeeper’s warning returns: your words will be heard, but they will not mean what you intend.

Lin Yue wonders if he has already broken a constraint without knowing. Or if the mortal world has its own constraints—ones that infect anything written.

He returns to Judge Ren and shows the shifting ink.

Judge Ren does not look surprised. He looks tired.

He says: 'That is why I copy testimonies by hand every day. The court records are alive. They learn what the powerful want them to learn.'

Lin Yue asks how to keep the truth stable.

Judge Ren hands him a small item: a plain seal carved from old wood, stained with soot.

He says the seal belonged to his father, who used it to mark famine ledgers so officials could not alter them without leaving scars.

Lin Yue cannot accept gifts under the constraints.

Judge Ren answers: 'Then call it a tool. Heaven sent you without miracles. So use mortal craft.'

Lin Yue stamps the wooden seal onto the shifting pages.

The ink screams—quietly, inside paper.

The writing stops changing.

But now every page bears a dark mark that feels like grief.

Lin Yue leaves the courthouse with stabilized evidence and a new understanding: truth in the mortal world is not discovered once.

It must be defended continuously, even from the paper that claims to hold it.

Chapter 2 ends with Lin Yue walking through the town as if through a battlefield, realizing his enemy is not a villain.

It is entropy dressed as administration.
content.id:
Hakim Ren mendorong bungkusan salinan kesaksian ke meja seperti orang mendorong beban yang sudah lelah ia pikul.

Lin Yue menerimanya dan langsung lega: bukti. Sesuatu yang bisa dibawa pulang ke Langit.

Di gang belakang pengadilan, Lin Yue membuka bungkusan itu untuk memastikan urutan halaman.

Tintanya bergeser.

Garis-garis kabur, lalu menegak menjadi kalimat baru.

Jantung Lin Yue meloncat. Ia membalik halaman. Nama berubah. Tanggal berubah. Kejahatan menjadi lebih bersih, lebih kecil, nyaris masuk akal.

Kesaksian itu sedang menulis ulang dirinya.

Lin Yue sadar horornya: korupsi bukan hanya di manusia. Ia di catatan. Di sistem. Di bahasa yang mengaku menggambarkan kebenaran.

Ia menutup halaman rapat dan menekan telapak tangannya, memaksa diam.

Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kakinya mengencang. Peringatan penjaga kembali: kata-katamu akan didengar, tetapi maknanya tidak akan seperti yang kau maksud.

Lin Yue bertanya-tanya apakah ia sudah melanggar batasan tanpa sadar. Atau dunia fana punya batasannya sendiri—yang menginfeksi apa pun yang ditulis.

Ia kembali ke Hakim Ren dan menunjukkan tinta yang bergerak.

Hakim Ren tidak terkejut. Ia lelah.

Ia berkata: 'Itulah kenapa aku menyalin kesaksian dengan tanganku setiap hari. Catatan pengadilan itu hidup. Ia belajar apa yang penguasa ingin ia pelajari.'

Lin Yue bertanya bagaimana menjaga kebenaran tetap stabil.

Hakim Ren memberinya sebuah benda kecil: segel polos dari kayu tua, berjelaga.

Ia berkata segel itu milik ayahnya, dipakai menandai buku kas kelaparan agar pejabat tak bisa mengubahnya tanpa meninggalkan luka.

Lin Yue tak boleh menerima hadiah menurut batasan.

Hakim Ren menjawab: 'Kalau begitu anggap saja alat. Langit mengirimmu tanpa mukjizat. Pakai kerajinan manusia.'

Lin Yue menstempel segel kayu itu pada halaman yang berubah.

Tintanya menjerit—pelan, di dalam kertas.

Tulisan berhenti berubah.

Namun kini setiap halaman punya bekas gelap yang terasa seperti duka.

Lin Yue meninggalkan pengadilan dengan bukti yang stabil dan pemahaman baru: kebenaran di dunia fana tidak ditemukan sekali.

Kebenaran harus dijaga terus-menerus, bahkan dari kertas yang mengaku menyimpannya.

Chapter 2 berakhir dengan Lin Yue berjalan melewati kota seperti melewati medan perang, sadar musuhnya bukan penjahat.

Musuhnya adalah peluruhan yang berdandan sebagai administrasi.

content.en:
Lin Yue delivers the question exactly: 'Where did the Mandate fail you?'

Judge Ren listens without flinching. His eyes stay steady, as if he has been waiting years for someone to ask the right question.

Then he says something Lin Yue did not expect.

He says: 'I will not answer Heaven.'

Lin Yue feels the invisible seal tighten. Not because the judge has broken a rule—because Lin Yue is about to.

He wants to persuade. He wants to argue that Heaven is listening. He wants to say this is an honor.

Judge Ren interrupts. He says Heaven has always listened. Heaven simply has not paid.

He explains: every time he punished a corrupt official, another official replaced him with a smile. Every time he protected a farmer, a new tax arrived wrapped in sacred language. Heaven’s order remained. Justice remained rare.

Judge Ren says if Heaven truly wants testimony, Heaven must do something first: admit that obedience without accountability is just organized harm.

Lin Yue asks what that would look like.

Judge Ren points to the clerk’s desks outside, to the piles of petitions, to the hunger in people’s faces. He says: 'It would look like consequences that reach upward.'

Lin Yue cannot promise that. He is a courier.

Judge Ren nods, almost kindly. He says: 'Then you are not here for truth. You are here for comfort.'

Lin Yue feels shame burn in his chest.

He writes down the refusal word for word.

Judge Ren watches him write and says one final sentence: 'Take my refusal back to your Emperor. If Heaven cannot bear a no, Heaven is not worthy of yes.'

Lin Yue leaves with no testimonies, no dramatic proof—only a recorded refusal.

The chapter ends with Lin Yue realizing that refusal is not a failure of the mission.

Refusal is the mission.

Because it tells Heaven what it most needs to learn: how to be answered without punishing the answerer.
content.id:
Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan itu persis: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'

Hakim Ren mendengarkan tanpa gentar. Matanya stabil, seakan ia sudah bertahun-tahun menunggu ada orang yang bertanya dengan benar.

Lalu ia mengatakan sesuatu yang tidak Lin Yue duga.

Ia berkata: 'Aku tidak akan menjawab Langit.'

Lin Yue merasakan segel tak terlihat mengencang. Bukan karena hakim melanggar aturan—melainkan karena Lin Yue akan.

Ia ingin membujuk. Ia ingin berargumen bahwa Langit sedang mendengar. Ia ingin bilang ini kehormatan.

Hakim Ren memotong. Ia berkata Langit selalu mendengar. Langit hanya tidak membayar.

Ia menjelaskan: setiap kali ia menghukum pejabat korup, pejabat lain menggantikan dengan senyum. Setiap kali ia melindungi petani, pajak baru datang dibungkus bahasa suci. Tertib Langit tetap ada. Adil tetap langka.

Hakim Ren berkata jika Langit benar ingin kesaksian, Langit harus melakukan sesuatu dulu: mengakui bahwa ketaatan tanpa pertanggungjawaban hanyalah luka yang tertata.

Lin Yue bertanya seperti apa itu.

Hakim Ren menunjuk meja-meja juru tulis di luar, tumpukan petisi, lapar di wajah orang. Ia berkata: 'Itu terlihat seperti konsekuensi yang naik ke atas.'

Lin Yue tidak bisa menjanjikannya. Ia cuma kurir.

Hakim Ren mengangguk, nyaris lembut. Ia berkata: 'Kalau begitu kau bukan datang untuk kebenaran. Kau datang untuk penghiburan.'

Lin Yue merasakan malu membakar dada.

Ia menulis penolakan itu kata demi kata.

Hakim Ren menatapnya menulis lalu berkata satu kalimat terakhir: 'Bawa penolakanku pulang ke Kaisarmu. Jika Langit tak sanggup menanggung kata tidak, Langit tidak layak mendapat kata ya.'

Lin Yue pergi tanpa kesaksian, tanpa bukti dramatis—hanya penolakan yang tercatat.

Bab ini berakhir dengan Lin Yue sadar bahwa penolakan bukan kegagalan misi.

Penolakan adalah misi.

Karena penolakan mengajari Langit hal yang paling perlu: bagaimana menerima jawaban tanpa menghukum si penjawab.

preview.en: With Judge Ren’s testimony in hand, Lin Yue tracks the second witness: a monk cast out for a scandal everyone repeats but no one understands.
preview.id: Dengan kesaksian Hakim Ren di tangan, Lin Yue mencari saksi kedua: biksu yang diusir karena skandal yang semua orang ulangi, tapi tak ada yang benar-benar mengerti.

content.en:
Lin Yue left the courthouse before sunset, not because the town had rejected him, but because it had accepted him too easily. Even honest testimony could become a commodity if it stayed in the same market too long.

Judge Ren’s bundle of copied statements sat in Lin Yue’s satchel like a second scroll—less sacred than the Emperor’s decree, yet heavier in a way Heaven would understand only after it bruised them.

The decree named the second witness plainly: a disgraced monk. Lin Yue had expected a temple. What he found instead were rumors.

In the inn by the river, people spoke the monk’s name the way they spoke about spoiled food: with disgust mixed with fascination. They did not say what he had done with certainty. They said what they enjoyed repeating.

Some said the monk stole donations.

Some said he broke vows.

Some said he sold blessings to the highest bidder.

And one old woman, half-drunk on rice wine, said the monk’s greatest sin was that he stopped being useful to the people who needed him to look holy.

Lin Yue listened without reacting. The constraints forbade interpretation, but they did not forbid pattern recognition. He began to sense that the monk’s scandal functioned like a shared story that allowed everyone else to feel clean.

He asked where the monk could be found.

The innkeeper pointed toward the foothills, toward an abandoned shrine road that no longer received pilgrims. 'If he’s alive,' the innkeeper said, 'he’s there, where the gods don’t have to see him.'

Lin Yue traveled at dawn. The road climbed into mist and pine shadow. Prayer flags hung in tatters. The air smelled of incense that had been burned long ago and never replaced.

Near midday he found a small hut built from scavenged wood beside a cracked stone statue whose face had been chiseled away. A man was sweeping the ground with a broom made of reeds.

The man wore a robe that used to be saffron but now looked like soil. His head was shaved, but not with temple neatness—shaved like someone who had no time for appearances.

Lin Yue bowed and spoke the monk’s name.

The man did not deny it. He did not confirm it either. He kept sweeping as if names were dust.

Lin Yue introduced himself as a courier with a question. He did not mention Heaven. He did not invoke miracles. He simply stated he carried an edict sealed by an authority that sought testimony.

The monk finally stopped sweeping. He looked at Lin Yue and smiled with a tired mouth.

'If you want testimony,' he said, 'you should have brought a knife. People only believe blood.'

Lin Yue answered calmly: 'This mission forbids knives.'

The monk’s smile sharpened. 'Then your masters finally understand something.'

Lin Yue delivered the question exactly: 'Where did the Mandate fail you?'

The monk laughed softly and sat on the hut’s threshold. He patted the ground beside him, not as hospitality, but as invitation to discomfort.

Lin Yue sat.

The monk began not with Heaven, but with hunger.

He said the temple had once been a refuge during famine. People came with empty hands and empty faces. The monks distributed rice, wrote petitions for the illiterate, and mediated disputes so blood would not be spilled.

Then officials noticed.

Officials began to visit the temple not to pray, but to perform care. They donated publicly. They took credit. They asked the abbot for endorsements.

The temple’s storehouse grew. The temple’s reputation grew. And the temple’s dependence grew too.

The monk said he watched the abbot compromise in small ways: accepting a donation with a condition, praising a magistrate who deserved suspicion, staying silent when a soldier dragged a farmer away.

'It was never one big betrayal,' the monk said. 'It was a thousand polite silences.'

Lin Yue wrote.

The monk’s scandal, he explained, happened the day he refused to be silent.

A boy had come to the temple beaten, accused of stealing grain from an official granary. The boy was guilty of theft, yes—but guilty because his siblings were starving.

The official demanded the monks hand the boy over.

The abbot hesitated.

The monk said he took the boy and hid him.

He broke the law.

He broke the temple’s agreement with power.

He broke the story that the temple was 'neutral.'

When the boy escaped, the official’s men searched the temple and found missing offerings, missing rice, missing records—because the monk had been diverting supplies quietly to families the temple claimed it could not help.

The abbot, cornered, chose the temple’s survival over the monk’s truth.

The monk was expelled publicly.

To keep the temple clean, they framed him as a thief.

The monk looked at Lin Yue and said, without bitterness, 'They needed me to be dirty so they could stay pure.'

Lin Yue’s pen slowed. The constraints demanded he record without judging, but his stomach tightened with a judge’s instinct anyway.

'So where did the Mandate fail you?' Lin Yue asked again, softer.

The monk pointed to the faceless statue.

'The Mandate failed when it made obedience look like virtue,' he said. 'It taught people to confuse clean hands with good hands. It taught priests to fear scandal more than suffering.'

Lin Yue wrote every word.

Then the monk added the sentence that broke Lin Yue’s certainty.

'And it failed when it taught rebels to pretend they are pure too,' the monk said. 'I hid the boy. I did good. But I also enjoyed being righteous. I enjoyed being the only honest man in the room.'

Lin Yue looked up.

The monk’s eyes were steady. 'If Heaven wants testimony, tell Heaven this: nobody is clean. Not the abbot. Not the official. Not me. If Heaven only listens to purity, Heaven will only hear liars.'

Lin Yue felt the Emperor’s warning from the first day: you will be tested by your need to be certain.

He realized certainty often wore the mask of cleanliness.

Before Lin Yue could ask more, the monk stood and resumed sweeping, as if the conversation had been confession and the broom was penance.

Lin Yue closed his notebook.

He did not offer comfort. He did not offer absolution. The mission did not permit it.

He thanked the monk with a bow and turned back toward the road.

As he walked away, Lin Yue understood Chapter 3’s lesson: the Mandate did not only fail through corruption. It also failed through the worship of appearances—through the demand that goodness must look spotless to count.

He now carried two testimonies that did not match.

Judge Ren demanded accountability from above.

The monk demanded honesty about the dirt in every hand.

Lin Yue looked at the sealed imperial scroll and wondered, for the first time, whether Heaven was prepared to hear answers that would not let Heaven remain beautiful.

Chapter 3 ends with Lin Yue descending from the foothills into the next valley, searching for the third witness—the woman who never prayed yet never lied—knowing that her truth might be the hardest, because it would come without religion to soften it.
content.id:
Lin Yue meninggalkan pengadilan sebelum matahari terbenam, bukan karena kota menolaknya, melainkan karena kota menerimanya terlalu mudah. Bahkan kesaksian jujur bisa berubah jadi komoditas jika terlalu lama tinggal di pasar yang sama.

Bungkusan salinan pernyataan dari Hakim Ren berada di tas Lin Yue seperti gulungan kedua—tidak sesuci maklumat Kaisar, tetapi lebih berat dengan cara yang hanya akan dimengerti Langit setelah ia memar.

Maklumat menyebut saksi kedua dengan jelas: biksu yang dipermalukan. Lin Yue mengira akan menemukan kuil. Yang ia temukan justru rumor.

Di penginapan tepi sungai, orang-orang menyebut nama biksu itu seperti menyebut makanan basi: jijik bercampur penasaran. Mereka tak menyebut apa yang ia lakukan dengan pasti. Mereka menyebut apa yang mereka nikmati untuk diulang.

Ada yang bilang ia mencuri donasi.

Ada yang bilang ia melanggar kaul.

Ada yang bilang ia menjual berkah pada penawar tertinggi.

Dan seorang nenek tua yang setengah mabuk arak beras berkata dosa terbesarnya adalah ia berhenti berguna bagi orang-orang yang butuh ia tampak suci.

Lin Yue mendengarkan tanpa bereaksi. Batasan melarang tafsir, tetapi tidak melarang membaca pola. Ia mulai merasa skandal biksu itu berfungsi seperti cerita bersama yang membuat semua orang lain merasa bersih.

Lin Yue bertanya di mana biksu itu bisa ditemukan.

Pemilik penginapan menunjuk ke kaki pegunungan, ke jalur kuil yang sudah ditinggalkan peziarah. 'Kalau dia masih hidup,' kata pemilik, 'dia di sana, tempat para dewa tidak perlu melihatnya.'

Lin Yue berangkat saat fajar. Jalan mendaki ke kabut dan bayang pinus. Bendera doa menggantung compang-camping. Udara berbau dupa yang dulu pernah dibakar, lalu tak pernah diganti.

Menjelang tengah hari ia menemukan gubuk kecil dari kayu bekas di samping patung batu retak yang wajahnya sudah dipahat hilang. Seorang pria sedang menyapu tanah dengan sapu dari ilalang.

Pria itu memakai jubah yang dulu kuning saffron, sekarang tampak seperti tanah. Kepalanya dicukur, tetapi bukan rapi ala kuil—dicukur seperti orang yang tak punya waktu untuk tampilan.

Lin Yue membungkuk dan menyebut nama biksu itu.

Pria itu tidak menyangkal. Ia juga tidak mengiyakan. Ia terus menyapu seakan nama hanya debu.

Lin Yue memperkenalkan diri sebagai kurir dengan sebuah pertanyaan. Ia tidak menyebut Langit. Ia tidak memanggil mukjizat. Ia hanya mengatakan ia membawa maklumat tersegel oleh otoritas yang meminta kesaksian.

Biksu itu akhirnya berhenti menyapu. Ia menatap Lin Yue dan tersenyum dengan mulut lelah.

'Kalau kau mau kesaksian,' katanya, 'seharusnya kau bawa pisau. Orang hanya percaya darah.'

Lin Yue menjawab tenang: 'Misi ini melarang pisau.'

Senyum biksu itu menajam. 'Berarti para penguasamu akhirnya mengerti sesuatu.'

Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan persis: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'

Biksu itu tertawa pelan lalu duduk di ambang gubuk. Ia menepuk tanah di sampingnya, bukan sebagai keramahan, melainkan undangan untuk tidak nyaman.

Lin Yue duduk.

Biksu itu memulai bukan dari Langit, melainkan dari lapar.

Ia berkata kuil dulu pernah jadi tempat berlindung saat kelaparan. Orang datang dengan tangan kosong dan wajah kosong. Para biksu membagi beras, menuliskan petisi bagi yang buta huruf, menengahi sengketa agar darah tidak tumpah.

Lalu pejabat memperhatikan.

Pejabat mulai datang bukan untuk berdoa, tetapi untuk tampil peduli. Mereka berdonasi di depan umum. Mereka mengambil kredit. Mereka meminta dukungan abbas.

Gudang kuil membesar. Reputasi kuil membesar. Dan ketergantungan kuil pun membesar.

Biksu itu berkata ia melihat abbas berkompromi sedikit demi sedikit: menerima donasi dengan syarat, memuji magistrat yang seharusnya dicurigai, diam ketika seorang serdadu menyeret petani.

'Itu bukan satu pengkhianatan besar,' kata biksu. 'Itu seribu kesunyian sopan.'

Lin Yue menulis.

Skandal biksu itu, ia jelaskan, terjadi pada hari ia menolak diam.

Seorang bocah datang dipukuli, dituduh mencuri gandum dari lumbung pejabat. Bocah itu memang mencuri—tetapi mencuri karena adik-adiknya kelaparan.

Pejabat menuntut para biksu menyerahkan bocah itu.

Abbas ragu.

Biksu itu berkata ia mengambil bocah itu dan menyembunyikannya.

Ia melanggar hukum.

Ia melanggar perjanjian kuil dengan kuasa.

Ia melanggar cerita bahwa kuil itu 'netral.'

Saat bocah itu lolos, orang-orang pejabat menggeledah kuil dan menemukan persembahan hilang, beras hilang, catatan hilang—karena biksu itu diam-diam mengalihkan persediaan untuk keluarga yang katanya tak bisa ditolong kuil.

Abbas yang terpojok memilih kelangsungan kuil daripada kebenaran biksu itu.

Biksu itu diusir secara publik.

Untuk membuat kuil tetap bersih, mereka menjebaknya sebagai pencuri.

Biksu itu menatap Lin Yue dan berkata tanpa pahit: 'Mereka butuh aku kotor supaya mereka bisa tetap suci.'

Pena Lin Yue melambat. Batasan meminta ia mencatat tanpa menghakimi, tetapi perutnya menegang oleh naluri hakim juga.

'Jadi di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?' tanya Lin Yue lagi, lebih pelan.

Biksu itu menunjuk patung tanpa wajah.

'Gagalnya Mandat saat ia membuat ketaatan tampak seperti kebajikan,' katanya. 'Ia mengajari orang mengira tangan bersih sama dengan tangan baik. Ia mengajari rohaniwan takut skandal lebih daripada takut penderitaan.'

Lin Yue menulis tiap kata.

Lalu biksu itu menambahkan kalimat yang memecah kepastian Lin Yue.

'Dan Mandat gagal saat ia mengajari pemberontak berpura-pura suci juga,' katanya. 'Aku menyembunyikan bocah itu. Aku berbuat baik. Tapi aku juga menikmati menjadi benar. Aku menikmati jadi satu-satunya orang jujur di ruangan.'

Lin Yue menatap.

Mata biksu itu stabil. 'Kalau Langit mau kesaksian, bilang pada Langit ini: tak ada yang bersih. Bukan abbas. Bukan pejabat. Bukan aku. Kalau Langit cuma mendengar kemurnian, Langit cuma akan mendengar pembohong.'

Lin Yue merasakan peringatan Kaisar hari pertama: kau akan diuji oleh kebutuhanmu untuk merasa pasti.

Ia sadar kepastian sering memakai topeng kebersihan.

Sebelum Lin Yue bertanya lagi, biksu itu berdiri dan kembali menyapu, seolah percakapan barusan adalah pengakuan dan sapu itu penebusan.

Lin Yue menutup catatannya.

Ia tidak menawarkan penghiburan. Ia tidak menawarkan pengampunan. Misi tidak mengizinkannya.

Ia berterima kasih dengan membungkuk lalu kembali ke jalan.

Saat berjalan turun, Lin Yue memahami pelajaran Chapter 3: Mandat tidak hanya gagal lewat korupsi. Mandat juga gagal lewat pemujaan pada tampilan—tuntutan bahwa kebaikan harus tampak mulus agar dihitung.

Kini ia memikul dua kesaksian yang tidak cocok.

Hakim Ren menuntut pertanggungjawaban dari atas.

Biksu itu menuntut kejujuran tentang lumpur di setiap tangan.

Lin Yue menatap gulungan maklumat tersegel dan bertanya untuk pertama kalinya: apakah Langit siap mendengar jawaban yang tidak membiarkan Langit tetap indah.

Chapter 3 berakhir ketika Lin Yue turun dari kaki pegunungan menuju lembah berikutnya, mencari saksi ketiga—perempuan yang tak pernah berdoa namun tak pernah berbohong—sadar bahwa kebenarannya mungkin paling sulit, karena datang tanpa agama untuk melunakkannya.

content.en:
Lin Yue expects the disgraced monk to defend himself, to reveal that the scandal was a misunderstanding or a political smear.

Instead, the monk smiles and says, 'They didn’t lie.'

Lin Yue’s pen pauses.

The monk admits he stole offerings.

Not once. Not by accident. Repeatedly.

He says he did it because he stopped believing the temple would ever choose the hungry over its reputation. He says he took the money and distributed it to starving families at night, but he also kept a portion for himself—small at first, then larger.

Lin Yue feels a cold disappointment: the witness is compromised.

The monk leans forward and says the compromise is the point.

He explains that everyone wanted a clean story. Either he was a saint framed by officials, or a villain exposed by justice. Both versions are comforting. Both versions protect the system.

The real story is harder: he did something right and wrong with the same hands, and the system used his wrong to erase his right.

Lin Yue delivers the decree’s question: 'Where did the Mandate fail you?'

The monk answers: 'It failed when it offered people only two masks—holy or wicked. It failed when it made mixed motives unspeakable.'

Lin Yue writes, stunned.

The monk adds that the Mandate also failed in the listeners. People preferred scandal to complexity because scandal makes judgment easy.

Then he turns the knife inward: 'And it failed in me, because I loved the role of secret savior. I loved being the one who knew better than the abbot. Pride can wear charity like perfume.'

Lin Yue realizes this testimony cannot be cleaned for Heaven. If he edits it, he becomes a liar.

He records it exactly.

Chapter 3 ends with Lin Yue leaving the hut sick with a new understanding: the Mandate may have failed not because it produced sinners, but because it trained everyone to demand simple stories—and simple stories are a kind of fraud.
content.id:
Lin Yue mengira biksu yang dipermalukan akan membela diri, mengungkap bahwa skandal itu salah paham atau fitnah politik.

Namun biksu itu tersenyum dan berkata, 'Mereka tidak bohong.'

Pena Lin Yue berhenti.

Biksu itu mengaku ia mencuri persembahan.

Bukan sekali. Bukan tak sengaja. Berulang.

Ia berkata ia melakukannya karena ia berhenti percaya kuil akan pernah memilih orang lapar dibanding reputasi. Ia berkata ia mengambil uang itu dan membagikannya diam-diam pada keluarga kelaparan di malam hari, tetapi ia juga menyisihkan sebagian untuk dirinya—kecil di awal, lalu makin besar.

Lin Yue merasakan kekecewaan dingin: saksi ini tercemar.

Biksu itu condong dan berkata justru 'tercemar' itulah intinya.

Ia menjelaskan semua orang ingin cerita yang bersih. Entah ia santo yang difitnah pejabat, atau penjahat yang dibongkar keadilan. Dua versi itu menenangkan. Dua-duanya melindungi sistem.

Cerita yang benar lebih sulit: ia melakukan benar dan salah dengan tangan yang sama, dan sistem memakai yang salah untuk menghapus yang benar.

Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan maklumat: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'
