Biksu itu menjawab: 'Mandat gagal ketika ia hanya menawarkan dua topeng—suci atau jahat. Mandat gagal ketika ia membuat motif campuran tak boleh diucapkan.'

Lin Yue menulis, terpukul.

Biksu itu menambahkan Mandat juga gagal pada para pendengar. Orang lebih suka skandal daripada kompleksitas karena skandal membuat menghakimi jadi mudah.

Lalu ia menusuk ke dalam: 'Dan Mandat gagal padaku, karena aku mencintai peran penyelamat rahasia. Aku suka merasa lebih benar dari abbas. Kesombongan bisa memakai amal seperti parfum.'

Lin Yue sadar kesaksian ini tidak bisa 'dibersihkan' untuk Langit. Kalau ia mengedit, ia jadi pembohong.

Ia mencatatnya persis.

Chapter 3 berakhir saat Lin Yue meninggalkan gubuk dengan perut mual dan pemahaman baru: Mandat mungkin gagal bukan karena ia menghasilkan pendosa, melainkan karena ia melatih semua orang menuntut cerita sederhana—dan cerita sederhana adalah bentuk penipuan.

content.en:
Lin Yue sits beside the monk and notices the faceless statue more carefully. The chisel marks are not random vandalism.

They are precise.

Someone removed the face cleanly, then smoothed the edges, as if the god’s identity itself was the offense.

When Lin Yue asks who did it, the monk says, 'I did.'

Lin Yue expects anger, but the monk’s voice is calm. He says the statue’s face was used as a tool—officials prayed to it before signing cruel orders, then pointed to the prayer as proof of virtue.

So the monk destroyed the face to stop the performance.

Lin Yue hears the risk in that logic. Destroying an icon does not destroy the hunger for icons.

As if to confirm this, the statue makes a sound.

A low vibration passes through the cracked stone, like a bell struck under water.

The monk goes still.

Lin Yue feels the invisible seal on his shadow tighten, warning him: miracles are forbidden.

But this is not his miracle.

A voice comes from the statue—thin, ancient, not commanding but weary. It asks one question: 'Do you want truth, or do you want a god-shaped excuse?'

Lin Yue cannot answer quickly.

The monk whispers that the statue is not a god anymore. It is a record. It has absorbed the prayers and lies offered to it for decades.

Lin Yue delivers the decree’s question to the statue, because the statue, too, has been used as a witness.

The statue answers with images instead of words: a clerk stamping papers over corpses, a priest blessing a soldier’s whip, a village rebuilding a shrine while its children starve.

Lin Yue writes what he can, hands shaking.

The monk says softly, 'Now you see why I live here. Holiness is not always divine. Sometimes it is just a place where lies gather.'

The statue falls silent again, reduced to stone.

Chapter 3 ends with Lin Yue leaving the hut convinced that testimony is not limited to mouths. In a world built on symbols, even broken symbols remember—and remembering can be more dangerous than speaking.
content.id:
Lin Yue duduk di samping biksu itu dan menatap patung tanpa wajah lebih teliti. Bekas pahatnya bukan vandalisme acak.

Bekasnya presisi.

Seseorang menghapus wajahnya bersih, lalu menghaluskan tepiannya, seakan identitas sang dewa sendiri adalah pelanggaran.

Saat Lin Yue bertanya siapa yang melakukannya, biksu itu berkata, 'Aku.'

Lin Yue mengira akan ada amarah, tetapi suara biksu itu tenang. Ia berkata wajah patung itu dipakai sebagai alat—para pejabat berdoa dulu sebelum menandatangani perintah kejam, lalu menunjuk doanya sebagai bukti kebajikan.

Maka biksu itu menghancurkan wajahnya untuk menghentikan pertunjukan.

Lin Yue mendengar bahayanya. Menghancurkan ikon tidak menghancurkan lapar akan ikon.

Seolah menegaskan, patung itu mengeluarkan bunyi.

Getaran rendah merambat di batu retak, seperti lonceng dipukul di bawah air.

Biksu itu mendadak diam.

Lin Yue merasakan segel tak terlihat pada bayangannya mengencang, memperingatkan: mukjizat dilarang.

Tetapi ini bukan mukjizatnya.

Suara keluar dari patung—tipis, kuno, tidak memerintah, melainkan letih. Ia bertanya satu hal: 'Kau ingin kebenaran, atau kau ingin alasan berbentuk dewa?'

Lin Yue tidak bisa menjawab cepat.

Biksu itu berbisik bahwa patung itu bukan dewa lagi. Ia catatan. Ia menyerap doa dan bohong yang ditujukan padanya selama puluhan tahun.

Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan maklumat pada patung itu, karena patung itu juga sudah dipakai sebagai saksi.

Patung menjawab dengan gambar, bukan kata: juru tulis menstempel berkas di atas mayat, rohaniwan memberkati cambuk serdadu, desa membangun ulang altar sementara anak-anaknya kelaparan.

Lin Yue menulis sebisanya, tangan gemetar.

Biksu itu berkata pelan, 'Sekarang kau mengerti kenapa aku tinggal di sini. Kesucian tidak selalu ilahi. Kadang ia cuma tempat bohong berkumpul.'

Patung itu sunyi lagi, kembali jadi batu.

Chapter 3 berakhir dengan Lin Yue pergi yakin bahwa kesaksian tidak hanya milik mulut. Dalam dunia yang dibangun simbol, bahkan simbol yang rusak mengingat—dan ingatan bisa lebih berbahaya daripada bicara.

content.en:
Lin Yue delivers the decree’s question. The monk listens, expressionless, then says, 'I’ll answer—if you do something first.'

Lin Yue’s seal tightens immediately. Conditions smell like bribes.

The monk points at the valley below. He says the temple that expelled him is still thriving. Pilgrims still come. Officials still donate. The poor still wait outside the gates.

He says he does not want revenge.

He wants a correction.

He wants Lin Yue to carry one sentence back to Heaven and have it recorded as fact: the monk’s real name.

Lin Yue is confused. The monk’s name is known.

The monk says the scandal erased it. People call him only 'thief,' 'traitor,' 'disgrace.' The role replaced the person.

He says, 'If Heaven wants truth, Heaven must stop letting labels eat faces.'

Lin Yue understands the demand: not salvation, not miracle—recognition.

But the constraints forbid promises.

Lin Yue answers carefully: he cannot promise Heaven will record anything. He can only promise to deliver words intact.

The monk nods. 'Then deliver this: my name is Mingyuan. And if Heaven can’t hold that, Heaven isn’t investigating a failure. Heaven is committing one.'

Lin Yue writes the name first, before anything else, as if anchoring the testimony to a human being.

Then the monk answers the question.

He describes the temple’s bargains, the abbot’s silences, the officials’ performances, and his own pride.

But his testimony keeps returning to names—who was blamed, who was protected, who was reduced to a rumor.

Lin Yue realizes the monk has turned the mission inside out. The Mandate did not fail only in law and weather.

It failed in language.

It failed when the world learned to replace people with stories about people.

Chapter 3 ends with Lin Yue leaving the hut holding a single name like a fragile artifact, aware that delivering it correctly might be more dangerous than delivering accusations.
content.id:
Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan maklumat. Biksu itu mendengar tanpa ekspresi, lalu berkata, 'Aku akan menjawab—kalau kau melakukan sesuatu dulu.'

Segel Lin Yue langsung mengencang. Syarat berbau suap.

Biksu itu menunjuk lembah di bawah. Ia berkata kuil yang mengusirnya masih makmur. Peziarah masih datang. Pejabat masih berdonasi. Orang miskin masih menunggu di luar gerbang.

Ia bilang ia tidak ingin balas dendam.

Ia ingin koreksi.

Ia ingin Lin Yue membawa satu kalimat pulang ke Langit dan memastikan itu dicatat sebagai fakta: nama asli si biksu.

Lin Yue bingung. Nama biksu itu dikenal.

Biksu itu berkata skandal menghapusnya. Orang hanya memanggilnya 'pencuri,' 'pengkhianat,' 'aib.' Peran menggantikan orang.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau Langit mau kebenaran, Langit harus berhenti membiarkan label memakan wajah.'

Lin Yue paham tuntutannya: bukan keselamatan, bukan mukjizat—pengakuan.

Namun batasan melarang janji.

Lin Yue menjawab hati-hati: ia tak bisa menjanjikan Langit akan mencatat apa pun. Ia hanya bisa menjanjikan menyampaikan kata-kata dengan utuh.

Biksu itu mengangguk. 'Kalau begitu sampaikan ini: namaku Mingyuan. Dan kalau Langit tak sanggup memegang itu, Langit bukan sedang menyelidiki kegagalan. Langit sedang melakukannya.'

Lin Yue menulis nama itu terlebih dulu, sebelum hal lain, seakan menambatkan kesaksian pada manusia.

Lalu biksu itu menjawab pertanyaan.

Ia menggambarkan tawar-menawar kuil, sunyi abbas, pertunjukan pejabat, dan kesombongannya sendiri.

Namun kesaksiannya terus kembali ke nama—siapa disalahkan, siapa dilindungi, siapa direduksi jadi rumor.

Lin Yue sadar biksu itu membalik misi dari dalam. Mandat tidak hanya gagal pada hukum dan cuaca.

Mandat gagal pada bahasa.

Mandat gagal saat dunia belajar mengganti manusia dengan cerita tentang manusia.

Chapter 3 berakhir dengan Lin Yue meninggalkan gubuk sambil memegang satu nama seperti artefak rapuh, sadar bahwa menyampaikannya dengan benar bisa lebih berbahaya daripada menyampaikan tuduhan.

title.en: A Witness Who Won’t Stay Put

content.en:
Lin Yue finishes recording the monk’s testimony and prepares to leave. The monk resumes sweeping, as if the conversation has been filed and closed.

But when Lin Yue reaches the road, he hears footsteps behind him.

The monk is following.

Lin Yue stops and asks why.

The monk says, 'Because you are carrying my words into a machine that will grind them into something useful. I want to see what they become.'

Lin Yue reminds him the mission forbids companions who could be mistaken for heavenly agents.

The monk laughs. 'No one will mistake me for Heaven.'

Lin Yue realizes the monk is right: disgrace is camouflage.

They travel together down the foothills. The monk does not preach. He watches.

In villages, people offer the monk food and then spit after he leaves. In markets, merchants greet him warmly and warn their children not to become him. The monk receives both kindness and contempt with the same face.

Lin Yue begins to understand the monk’s real expertise: he knows how moral stories are used to manage crowds.

The monk points out small hypocrisies Lin Yue would miss—how temple charity is timed for festivals, how officials donate in public but collect in private, how gossip travels faster than hunger.

Lin Yue feels his own certainty erode. The monk’s presence is not comfort.

It is friction.

At night, Lin Yue asks the monk if he regrets anything.

The monk answers, 'I regret the pleasure I took in being right. That pleasure is a seed. It grows into cruelty.'

Lin Yue records the sentence.

But now the sentence is not testimony about the past. It is warning about Lin Yue.

Chapter 3 ends with the monk still walking beside him as they enter the next valley, and Lin Yue realizes the mission has changed shape: he is no longer collecting witnesses.

He is accumulating consequences.
content.id:
Lin Yue selesai mencatat kesaksian biksu itu dan bersiap pergi. Biksu itu kembali menyapu, seakan percakapan sudah diberkas dan ditutup.

Namun ketika Lin Yue sampai di jalan, ia mendengar langkah di belakang.

Biksu itu mengikuti.

Lin Yue berhenti dan bertanya kenapa.

Biksu itu berkata, 'Karena kau membawa kata-kataku ke mesin yang akan menggilingnya jadi sesuatu yang berguna. Aku mau melihat ia berubah jadi apa.'

Lin Yue mengingatkan bahwa misi melarang rekan perjalanan yang bisa disangka agen Langit.

Biksu itu tertawa. 'Tak ada yang akan menyangka aku Langit.'

Lin Yue sadar biksu itu benar: aib adalah kamuflase.

Mereka berjalan bersama turun dari kaki gunung. Biksu itu tidak berkhotbah. Ia mengamati.

Di desa, orang memberi makanan lalu meludah setelah biksu itu pergi. Di pasar, pedagang menyapa ramah lalu memperingatkan anaknya agar tidak menjadi seperti dia. Biksu itu menerima kebaikan dan hinaan dengan wajah yang sama.

Lin Yue mulai mengerti keahlian biksu yang sebenarnya: ia paham bagaimana cerita moral dipakai untuk mengatur kerumunan.

Biksu itu menunjuk kemunafikan kecil yang Lin Yue takkan lihat—amal kuil yang waktunya disetel mengikuti festival, pejabat yang menyumbang di depan umum tapi memungut diam-diam, gosip yang bergerak lebih cepat dari lapar.

Lin Yue merasakan kepastian di dalamnya tergerus. Kehadiran biksu itu bukan penghiburan.

Itu gesekan.

Malam hari, Lin Yue bertanya apakah biksu itu menyesal.

Biksu itu menjawab, 'Aku menyesal kenikmatan saat merasa paling benar. Kenikmatan itu benih. Ia tumbuh jadi kejam.'

Lin Yue mencatat kalimat itu.

Namun kini kalimat itu bukan lagi kesaksian masa lalu. Ia peringatan untuk Lin Yue.

Chapter 3 berakhir dengan biksu itu masih berjalan di sampingnya saat mereka memasuki lembah berikutnya, dan Lin Yue sadar misi telah berubah bentuk: ia tidak lagi mengumpulkan saksi.

Ia sedang menimbun konsekuensi.

preview.en: Lin Yue reaches the third witness—an uncelebrated woman whose honesty has cost her everything—and learns that truth can be moral without being religious.
preview.id: Lin Yue menemukan saksi ketiga—perempuan biasa yang kejujurannya membuatnya kehilangan banyak hal—dan belajar bahwa kebenaran bisa bermoral tanpa harus religius.

content.en:
The next valley was wider, flatter, and louder. Where the monk’s foothills held silence like a vow, this place held noise like commerce. Lin Yue walked along a river road lined with carts, vendors, and travelers whose faces carried the dust of many small disappointments.

The decree’s third witness was described in a way that felt almost insulting in its simplicity: a woman who never prayed yet never lied.

Lin Yue had expected a rebel, an atheist philosopher, or someone famous for defying temples.

Instead he found a laundress.

Her name was Wei Lan. People said it with a certain tone—half admiration, half irritation—as if even speaking her name required a moral posture.

Lin Yue asked for her in a riverside market. Several merchants pointed him toward the dye houses with the same gesture they used to point toward trouble.

When Lin Yue reached the dye yard, he found a woman knee-deep in basins of dark water, wringing cloth with hands that looked too strong for someone who had ever been allowed to rest.

She looked up at him once, quickly, and returned to her work.

Lin Yue introduced himself as a courier. He did not mention Heaven. He had learned by now that invoking the divine could be a way of stealing sincerity.

Wei Lan said, without looking: 'Couriers bring orders. I don’t take orders.'

Lin Yue replied: 'This is a question, not an order.'

That earned him her eyes.

They were not hostile. They were measuring—like a person checking whether a tool will break under honest use.

Lin Yue asked if she was Wei Lan.

She said yes. Then she added, flatly: 'And no, I don’t pray. If you came to fix that, go to the temple. They like fixing faces.'

Lin Yue felt the monk’s lesson echo: holiness can become a performance that hides harm.

He bowed and said he came for testimony.

Wei Lan laughed once. It was not cruel laughter. It was the laugh of someone who has been asked to justify herself too many times.

'Testimony,' she repeated. 'Then you’re late. The courts already asked. The priests already asked. The guild already asked. They all wanted my words to mean what they needed.'

Lin Yue told her he would record her answer without interpretation.

Wei Lan wiped her hands on her apron and walked to the edge of the yard, where a broken wall overlooked the river.

She did not invite him to sit. She simply began.

She said she learned early that prayers often functioned like insurance. People prayed before lying, as if the lie needed a witness. People prayed after cheating, as if the prayer could make theft look like necessity.

So she stopped praying.

Not because she hated gods.

Because she hated the way people used gods to decorate their choices.

Lin Yue listened. His seal did not tighten. This was not blasphemy. It was ethics.

Then Lin Yue delivered the decree’s question exactly: 'Where did the Mandate fail you?'

Wei Lan’s answer was not a theory. It was a history.

She said the Mandate failed her first when her father died in a workplace accident and the guild recorded it as 'misfortune' instead of negligence. There was no compensation because no one had to be accountable to misfortune.

It failed again when she reported a foreman who demanded payment for 'safety checks' that were never performed. The foreman was not punished. She was reassigned to the worst work.

It failed again when a temple priest told her suffering was purification, and suggested she was arrogant for wanting justice. The priest, she said, had never carried wet cloth in winter.

Wei Lan looked at Lin Yue sharply and said: 'The Mandate failed because it taught powerful men a trick. They can call their choices 'fate' and your pain 'lesson.' Then they don’t have to change.'

Lin Yue wrote the sentence carefully.

Wei Lan continued. She said she never lied because lies invite hierarchy. A lie says: I decide what you are allowed to know. Truth, even ugly truth, treats others as equals.

Lin Yue realized he had never heard honesty defined as equality.

Wei Lan said her honesty cost her. Customers stopped coming because she refused to flatter them. Merchants disliked her because she refused to understate debts. Priests disliked her because she refused to pretend belief.

She said she was not heroic.

She was simply tired of being managed.

Lin Yue asked—carefully, because it could have sounded like judgment—why she never prayed.

Wei Lan answered: 'Because prayer became a way to skip responsibility. If the gods are always blamed or praised, humans never have to look at each other and admit what they did.'

Lin Yue felt the three testimonies inside him collide.

Judge Ren demanded that consequences reach upward.

The monk demanded that purity stop being a requirement for truth.

Wei Lan demanded that fate stop being used as a shield.

Lin Yue looked down at the Emperor’s sealed scroll and wondered whether Heaven’s Mandate was failing because it had become too easy to hide behind it.

Before Lin Yue could close his notebook, Wei Lan asked him a question in return.

'Who sent you?' she asked.

Lin Yue felt the invisible constraint tighten around his ankles—not because he wanted to reveal Heaven, but because he wanted to be believed.

He said, truthfully but carefully: 'An authority that can punish itself if it chooses.'

Wei Lan snorted. 'Then tell it to choose.'

She turned back to her basins.

Lin Yue stood there, holding the notebook like a weight, and understood Chapter 4’s lesson: some of the strongest testimony comes from people who have no investment in holiness.

They cannot be bribed with heaven.

They cannot be frightened with hell.

They only ask that human beings stop lying to each other.

As Lin Yue left the dye yard, the town’s temple bell rang in the distance. He noticed, with a strange clarity, that the bell was not calling Wei Lan.

It was calling everyone else.

Chapter 4 ends with Lin Yue walking along the river at dusk, carrying all three testimonies and a new fear: the hardest part will not be delivering mortals’ truth to Heaven.

The hardest part will be whether Heaven is willing to let itself be described by people it cannot control.
content.id:
Lembah berikutnya lebih lebar, lebih datar, dan lebih berisik. Jika kaki gunung biksu menyimpan sunyi seperti kaul, tempat ini menyimpan riuh seperti dagang. Lin Yue berjalan di jalan sungai yang dipenuhi gerobak, pedagang, dan pelancong yang wajahnya membawa debu dari banyak kekecewaan kecil.

Saksi ketiga dalam maklumat digambarkan dengan cara yang hampir terasa menghina karena terlalu sederhana: seorang perempuan yang tak pernah berdoa namun tak pernah berbohong.

Lin Yue mengira ia akan menemukan pemberontak, filsuf ateis, atau tokoh terkenal yang menantang kuil.

Ternyata yang ia temukan adalah tukang cuci.

Namanya Wei Lan. Orang menyebutnya dengan nada tertentu—setengah kagum, setengah jengkel—seolah menyebut namanya saja butuh sikap moral.

Lin Yue menanyakannya di pasar tepi sungai. Beberapa pedagang menunjuk ke arah rumah pewarnaan dengan gestur yang sama seperti menunjuk ke arah masalah.

Saat Lin Yue sampai di halaman pewarnaan, ia menemukan seorang perempuan setinggi lutut di dalam bak air gelap, memeras kain dengan tangan yang terlalu kuat untuk orang yang pernah diberi waktu istirahat.

Ia menatap Lin Yue sekali, cepat, lalu kembali bekerja.

Lin Yue memperkenalkan diri sebagai kurir. Ia tidak menyebut Langit. Ia sudah belajar bahwa memanggil yang ilahi bisa jadi cara mencuri ketulusan.

Wei Lan berkata tanpa menoleh: 'Kurir membawa perintah. Aku tidak menerima perintah.'

Lin Yue menjawab: 'Ini pertanyaan, bukan perintah.'

Baru itu Wei Lan menatapnya.

Tatapannya tidak bermusuhan. Tatapannya mengukur—seperti orang memeriksa apakah sebuah alat akan patah jika dipakai jujur.

Lin Yue bertanya apakah ia Wei Lan.

Ia mengangguk. Lalu ia menambahkan datar: 'Dan tidak, aku tidak berdoa. Kalau kau datang untuk memperbaiki itu, pergi ke kuil. Mereka suka memperbaiki wajah.'

Lin Yue merasakan pelajaran biksu bergema: kesucian bisa menjadi pertunjukan yang menutupi luka.

Ia membungkuk dan berkata ia datang untuk kesaksian.

Wei Lan tertawa sekali. Itu bukan tawa kejam. Itu tawa orang yang sudah terlalu sering diminta membenarkan dirinya.

'Kesaksian,' ia mengulang. 'Kalau begitu kau terlambat. Pengadilan sudah tanya. Pendeta sudah tanya. Serikat sudah tanya. Mereka semua ingin kata-kataku berarti seperti yang mereka butuh.'

Lin Yue berkata ia akan mencatat jawabannya tanpa tafsir.

Wei Lan mengusap tangannya pada celemek lalu berjalan ke tepi halaman, ke tembok retak yang menghadap sungai.

Ia tidak mengundang Lin Yue duduk. Ia langsung mulai.

Ia berkata ia belajar sejak kecil bahwa doa sering berfungsi seperti asuransi. Orang berdoa sebelum berbohong, seakan bohong butuh saksi. Orang berdoa setelah menipu, seakan doa bisa membuat pencurian tampak seperti kebutuhan.

Maka ia berhenti berdoa.

Bukan karena ia membenci dewa.

Melainkan karena ia membenci cara orang memakai dewa sebagai hiasan pilihan.

Lin Yue mendengar. Segelnya tidak mengencang. Ini bukan hujat. Ini etika.

Lalu Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan maklumat persis: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'

Jawaban Wei Lan bukan teori. Itu riwayat.

Ia berkata Mandat gagal pertama saat ayahnya mati dalam kecelakaan kerja dan serikat mencatatnya sebagai 'kemalangan', bukan kelalaian. Tidak ada ganti rugi karena tidak ada yang perlu bertanggung jawab pada kemalangan.

Mandat gagal lagi saat ia melaporkan mandor yang meminta uang untuk 'cek keselamatan' yang tak pernah dilakukan. Mandor tidak dihukum. Wei Lan dipindah ke kerja paling buruk.

Mandat gagal lagi saat seorang pendeta kuil berkata penderitaan adalah pemurnian, dan menyiratkan Wei Lan sombong karena ingin keadilan. Pendeta itu, katanya, tidak pernah mengangkat kain basah di musim dingin.

Wei Lan menatap Lin Yue tajam dan berkata: 'Mandat gagal karena ia mengajari lelaki berkuasa satu trik. Mereka bisa menyebut pilihan mereka “takdir” dan sakitmu “pelajaran”. Lalu mereka tidak perlu berubah.'

Lin Yue menulis kalimat itu hati-hati.

Wei Lan melanjutkan. Ia berkata ia tak pernah berbohong karena bohong mengundang hierarki. Bohong berkata: aku yang memutuskan apa yang boleh kau tahu. Kebenaran, bahkan kebenaran jelek, memperlakukan orang lain sebagai setara.

Lin Yue sadar ia belum pernah mendengar kejujuran didefinisikan sebagai kesetaraan.

Wei Lan berkata kejujurannya ada harga. Pelanggan berhenti datang karena ia menolak merayu. Pedagang tak suka karena ia menolak mengecilkan utang. Pendeta tak suka karena ia menolak pura-pura percaya.

Ia berkata ia bukan pahlawan.

Ia cuma lelah terus-menerus dikelola.

Lin Yue bertanya—hati-hati agar tak terdengar menghakimi—kenapa ia tak pernah berdoa.

Wei Lan menjawab: 'Karena doa jadi cara melompati tanggung jawab. Kalau dewa selalu disalahkan atau dipuji, manusia tidak perlu saling menatap dan mengaku apa yang mereka lakukan.'

Di dalam Lin Yue, tiga kesaksian bertabrakan.

Hakim Ren menuntut konsekuensi naik ke atas.

Biksu menuntut kemurnian berhenti jadi syarat kebenaran.

Wei Lan menuntut takdir berhenti dipakai sebagai tameng.

Lin Yue menatap gulungan maklumat tersegel dan bertanya apakah Mandat Langit gagal karena terlalu mudah dipakai orang untuk bersembunyi.

Sebelum Lin Yue menutup catatan, Wei Lan bertanya balik.

'Siapa yang mengirimmu?' tanyanya.

Lin Yue merasakan batasan tak terlihat mengencang di pergelangan kaki—bukan karena ia ingin membuka Langit, melainkan karena ia ingin dipercaya.

Ia menjawab, jujur tapi hati-hati: 'Otoritas yang bisa menghukum dirinya sendiri kalau ia memilih.'

Wei Lan mendengus. 'Kalau begitu suruh ia memilih.'

Wei Lan kembali ke bak-baknya.

Lin Yue berdiri memegang buku catatan seperti beban, dan paham pelajaran Chapter 4: sebagian kesaksian terkuat datang dari orang yang tidak punya investasi pada kesucian.

Mereka tak bisa disuap dengan surga.

Mereka tak bisa ditakuti dengan neraka.

Mereka hanya meminta manusia berhenti saling bohong.

Saat Lin Yue meninggalkan halaman pewarnaan, lonceng kuil kota berdentang dari jauh. Ia menyadari dengan jernih bahwa lonceng itu tidak memanggil Wei Lan.

Lonceng itu memanggil semua orang lain.

Chapter 4 berakhir ketika Lin Yue berjalan menyusuri sungai saat senja, membawa tiga kesaksian dan ketakutan baru: bagian tersulit bukan menyampaikan kebenaran manusia pada Langit.

Bagian tersulit adalah apakah Langit bersedia membiarkan dirinya didefinisikan oleh orang-orang yang tidak bisa ia kendalikan.

content.en:
Wei Lan is known in the town as the woman who never prays and never lies. The description has hardened around her like a second skin she did not ask to wear.

When Lin Yue comes with his question, she answers as she always has: directly, without ornament, without piety.

But later that night, as the dye yard closes and the river swallows the day’s colors, a small crisis arrives.

A young girl from the dye house is accused of stealing silver thread. The overseer is certain. The girl is shaking. The thread is missing.

Everyone turns to Wei Lan.

Truth has become her job.

Wei Lan knows the girl did take the thread—but she also knows why. She saw the bruises on the girl’s arms, heard the way the overseer spoke when no one else was listening. The girl was hoarding scraps to sell for escape.

The overseer demands that Wei Lan confirm the theft.

Silence stretches.

Then Wei Lan lies.

'No,' she says. 'I saw nothing.'

The yard exhales. Accusation dissolves into uneasy relief. The girl is spared punishment—for now.

Lin Yue feels the lie like a crack in a bell. The town’s one fixed point has shifted.

Afterward, he asks her why.

Wei Lan answers without apology: 'Because sometimes the truth is used as a weapon by people who don’t deserve it.'

He tells her the description Heaven gave: never prayed, never lied.

She shrugs. 'Descriptions are cages. Maybe Heaven should stop making cages out of people.'

Lin Yue realizes the Mandate has failed in a new way: by turning human consistency into doctrine.

He writes the lie into his record, even though it ruins the symmetry of Heaven’s story.

Chapter 4 ends with Lin Yue understanding that a single compassionate lie can reveal more about justice than a lifetime of clean reputation—and that if Heaven wants reality, it must accept witnesses who sometimes break their own rules.
content.id:
Di kota, Wei Lan dikenal sebagai perempuan yang tak pernah berdoa dan tak pernah berbohong. Deskripsi itu mengeras di sekelilingnya seperti kulit kedua yang tak pernah ia minta.

Saat Lin Yue datang dengan pertanyaannya, ia menjawab seperti biasa: langsung, tanpa hiasan, tanpa kesalehan.

Namun malam itu, ketika halaman pewarnaan tutup dan sungai menelan warna-warna hari, krisis kecil datang.

Seorang gadis muda dari rumah pewarna dituduh mencuri benang perak. Mandor yakin. Gadis itu gemetar. Benang itu hilang.

Semua menoleh pada Wei Lan.

Kebenaran telah menjadi pekerjaannya.

Wei Lan tahu gadis itu memang mengambil benang—tetapi ia juga tahu alasannya. Ia melihat memar di lengan gadis itu, mendengar cara mandor berbicara saat tak ada yang lain. Gadis itu menimbun sisa untuk dijual demi kabur.

Mandor menuntut Wei Lan mengakui pencurian.

Sunyi menegang.

Lalu Wei Lan berbohong.

'Tidak,' katanya. 'Aku tidak melihat apa-apa.'

Halaman mengembus napas. Tuduhan larut jadi lega yang tak nyaman. Gadis itu luput dari hukuman—untuk saat ini.

Lin Yue merasakan bohong itu seperti retak di lonceng. Titik tetap kota bergeser.

Sesudahnya, ia bertanya kenapa.

Wei Lan menjawab tanpa menyesal: 'Karena kadang kebenaran dipakai sebagai senjata oleh orang yang tak pantas memegangnya.'

Ia memberitahu Wei Lan deskripsi dari Langit: tak pernah berdoa, tak pernah berbohong.

Wei Lan mengangkat bahu. 'Deskripsi adalah sangkar. Mungkin Langit harus berhenti membuat sangkar dari manusia.'

Lin Yue sadar Mandat gagal dengan cara baru: dengan mengubah konsistensi manusia menjadi doktrin.

Ia menulis kebohongan itu ke catatannya, meski merusak kerapian cerita Langit.

Chapter 4 berakhir ketika Lin Yue paham bahwa satu kebohongan penuh belas kasih bisa mengungkap lebih banyak tentang keadilan daripada seumur hidup reputasi bersih—dan jika Langit ingin kenyataan, Langit harus menerima saksi yang kadang melanggar aturannya sendiri.

content.en:
Wei Lan tells Lin Yue she never prays because prayer became a way to skip responsibility. She believes it. She has lived by it.

Days after his visit, a flood warning comes down the river.

The town is not ready. The officials argue about budgets, the priests organize processions, and the merchants fight over whose warehouses should be protected first.

Wei Lan moves her basins, lifts what she can, and watches the sky darken with a practical dread.

Then she does something she has not done since childhood.

She prays.

Not at the temple. Not before an altar.

She mutters a single sentence into the roar of the coming storm: 'If anyone is listening, make me useful.'

There is no sign. No light, no voice, no answered thunder.

What happens instead is smaller and more dangerous.

She begins shouting orders.

She tells neighbors which doors to brace, where to stack sandbags, which alleys will channel water. People listen, not because of heaven, but because Wei Lan has never lied to them.

The flood hits. It is bad.

It is less bad than it could have been.

Later, drenched and exhausted, Wei Lan realizes her prayer was answered in the only way that fits her life: not by changing the river, but by changing what she allowed herself to do.

When Lin Yue returns through the valley days afterward, he hears that the town credits the temple’s procession for their relative safety.

Wei Lan does not correct them.

When Lin Yue asks if she still never prays, she says, 'I prayed once. I asked to be useful. The rest was just work.'

He records this as part of her testimony.

Chapter 4 ends with Lin Yue recognizing that the boundary between faith and action is thinner than Heaven’s categories allow—and that the Mandate may have failed by insisting that help must look sacred to count.
content.id:
Wei Lan berkata pada Lin Yue ia tak pernah berdoa karena doa menjadi cara melompati tanggung jawab. Ia yakin. Ia hidup dengan prinsip itu.

Beberapa hari setelah kunjungan Lin Yue, peringatan banjir datang menyusuri sungai.

Kota belum siap. Pejabat berdebat soal anggaran, pendeta mengatur arak-arakan, pedagang bertengkar tentang gudang siapa yang harus dilindungi dulu.

Wei Lan memindah bak-baknya, mengangkat apa yang bisa ia selamatkan, dan menatap langit yang menggelap dengan cemas yang praktis.

Lalu ia melakukan sesuatu yang tak pernah ia lakukan sejak kecil.

Ia berdoa.

Bukan di kuil. Bukan di depan altar.

Ia bergumam satu kalimat ke dalam deru badai yang datang: 'Kalau ada yang mendengar, buat aku berguna.'

Tidak ada tanda. Tak ada cahaya, suara, atau guntur jawaban.

Yang terjadi justru lebih kecil dan lebih berbahaya.

Ia mulai berteriak memberi perintah.

Ia memberitahu tetangga pintu mana yang harus diganjal, di mana menumpuk karung pasir, gang mana yang akan jadi saluran air. Orang-orang mendengarkan, bukan karena surga, tetapi karena Wei Lan tak pernah berbohong kepada mereka.

Banjir datang. Banjir itu buruk.

Namun tidak seburuk yang mungkin.

Belakangan, basah kuyup dan letih, Wei Lan sadar doanya dijawab dengan satu-satunya cara yang pas dengan hidupnya: bukan dengan mengubah sungai, tetapi dengan mengubah apa yang ia izinkan dirinya lakukan.

Saat Lin Yue kembali melewati lembah beberapa hari kemudian, ia mendengar kota memberi kredit pada arak-arakan kuil untuk keselamatan relatif mereka.

Wei Lan tidak membantah.

Saat Lin Yue bertanya apakah ia masih 'tak pernah berdoa', ia menjawab, 'Aku sekali berdoa. Aku minta dibuat berguna. Sisanya ya kerja.'

Lin Yue mencatat ini sebagai bagian dari kesaksiannya.

Chapter 4 berakhir ketika Lin Yue menyadari batas antara iman dan tindakan lebih tipis dari kategori Langit, dan Mandat mungkin gagal karena menuntut pertolongan harus tampak suci dulu baru dianggap sah.

content.en:
When Lin Yue delivers the decree’s question, Wei Lan listens, eyes narrowed, hands still wet with dye.

He asks: 'Where did the Mandate fail you?'

She wipes her hands, walks to the broken wall by the river, and stares at the water for a long time.

Then she says, 'No.'

Lin Yue waits, thinking she has not understood. He repeats the question.

She shakes her head. 'I won’t answer.'

He feels the invisible seal tighten. Refusal was anticipated in the mission, but he had not felt it this sharply until now.

He asks why.

Wei Lan answers: 'Because every time I speak, someone with more power than me takes my words and turns them into their story. Courts, priests, guilds, husbands. Why should Heaven be different?'

Lin Yue promises to record her answer exactly.

She laughs. 'Record this, then: I refuse.'

Silence stretches between them.

Lin Yue realizes the refusal itself is an answer. The Mandate has failed so deeply that its victims no longer trust any listener, even one who claims to come from a place that can punish itself.

He writes in his notebook: 'Witness declined to testify. Reason: words are not safe.'

Wei Lan nods once, as if that is the first honest transaction she has seen all week.

Chapter 4 ends with Lin Yue leaving the dye yard carrying no anecdotes, no narratives, only the weight of a closed mouth—and understanding that sometimes the most accurate report of injustice is not a story.

It is the absence of one.
content.id:
Ketika Lin Yue menyampaikan pertanyaan maklumat, Wei Lan mendengarkan, mata menyipit, tangan masih basah oleh pewarna.

Ia bertanya: 'Di mana Mandat gagal untukmu?'

Wei Lan mengusap tangan, berjalan ke tembok retak di tepi sungai, dan menatap air lama sekali.

Lalu ia berkata, 'Tidak.'

Lin Yue menunggu, mengira Wei Lan belum paham. Ia mengulang pertanyaan.

Ia menggeleng. 'Aku tidak akan menjawab.'

Segel tak terlihat mengencang. Penolakan sudah diantisipasi dalam misi, tetapi belum pernah terasa setajam ini.

Lin Yue bertanya kenapa.

Wei Lan menjawab: 'Karena setiap kali aku bicara, orang yang lebih berkuasa mengambil kata-kataku dan mengubahnya jadi cerita mereka. Pengadilan, pendeta, serikat, suami. Kenapa Langit harus berbeda?'

Lin Yue berjanji akan mencatat jawabannya persis.

Wei Lan tertawa. 'Kalau begitu catat ini: aku menolak.'

Sunyi meregang di antara mereka.

Lin Yue sadar penolakan itu sendiri adalah jawaban. Mandat gagal begitu dalam sampai korbannya tidak lagi percaya pada pendengar mana pun, bahkan yang mengaku datang dari tempat yang bisa menghukum dirinya sendiri.

Ia menulis di buku catatan: 'Saksi menolak memberi kesaksian. Alasan: kata-kata tidak aman.'

Wei Lan mengangguk sekali, seolah itu transaksi paling jujur yang ia lihat minggu itu.

Chapter 4 berakhir ketika Lin Yue meninggalkan halaman pewarnaan tanpa anekdot, tanpa narasi, hanya beban mulut yang tertutup—dan paham bahwa kadang laporan paling akurat tentang ketidakadilan bukan cerita.

Laporan itu adalah ketiadaan cerita.

content.en:
Lin Yue offers Wei Lan the question carefully, expecting anger at officials, guilds, priests.

He does not expect her to look up.

After listing the ways the Mandate has been used to excuse harm, Wei Lan lifts her chin toward the empty sky.

She says, 'If your Mandate says this is order, then your Mandate is a lie.'

Lin Yue feels the words slice through his training. Accusing Heaven directly is more than honesty. It is treason by most definitions he knows.

The invisible seal around his ankles tightens, not as punishment, but as if reminding him that he is not here to intervene.

He asks her to explain.

Wei Lan says fate is not neutral. It always seems to fall in the same direction: down, onto the backs of the same people, in the same streets.

She says, 'If the sky wanted justice, it had enough storms and seasons to make a point. Instead, it lets men blame it for what they choose.'

Lin Yue hears, beneath the accusation, something that is not quite blasphemy.

It is disappointment.

He realizes she is not sure the Jade Emperor exists.

She is very sure that if he does, he has been lazy.

Lin Yue writes her words exactly, hand trembling.

He knows that when he returns, some in Heaven will say her testimony is insolent, impure, unworthy of record.

But the decree did not ask for polite answers.

It asked where the Mandate failed.

Chapter 4 ends with Lin Yue deciding that if Heaven cannot bear even this accusation, then the investigation is theater—and Wei Lan has, unintentionally, become Heaven’s most necessary critic.
content.id:
Lin Yue mengajukan pertanyaan pada Wei Lan hati-hati, mengira ia akan marah pada pejabat, serikat, pendeta.

Ia tidak mengira Wei Lan akan menatap ke atas.

Sesudah merinci bagaimana Mandat dipakai membenarkan luka, Wei Lan mengangkat dagu ke langit kosong.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau Mandat kalian bilang ini tertib, berarti Mandat kalian bohong.'

Kata-kata itu membelah latihan Lin Yue. Menuduh Langit langsung lebih dari sekadar jujur. Dalam hampir semua definisi yang ia tahu, itu pengkhianatan.

Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kaki mengencang, bukan menghukum, tetapi seolah mengingatkan bahwa ia tidak datang untuk campur tangan.

Lin Yue meminta Wei Lan menjelaskan.

Wei Lan berkata takdir tidak netral. Takdir selalu jatuh ke arah yang sama: ke punggung orang yang sama, di jalan yang sama.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau langit mau keadilan, ia punya cukup badai dan musim untuk membuat pernyataan. Tapi langit membiarkan lelaki menyalahkannya atas apa yang mereka pilih.'

Lin Yue mendengar, di bawah tuduhan itu, sesuatu yang bukan sekadar hujat.

Itu kekecewaan.

Ia sadar Wei Lan tidak yakin Kaisar Giok ada.

Ia sangat yakin kalau Kaisar ada, Kaisar malas.

Lin Yue menulis kata-kata itu persis, tangan bergetar.

Ia tahu saat ia kembali, beberapa di Langit akan berkata kesaksian ini lancang, tidak murni, tidak layak dicatat.

Namun maklumat tidak meminta jawaban sopan.

Maklumat bertanya di mana Mandat gagal.

Chapter 4 berakhir ketika Lin Yue memutuskan bahwa jika Langit tak sanggup menanggung tuduhan ini, penyelidikan ini hanyalah sandiwara—dan Wei Lan, tanpa sengaja, menjadi kritikus yang paling dibutuhkan Langit.

preview.en: Lin Yue begins the ascent back to Heaven, discovers that mortal testimony reshapes him—and finds that Heaven has been changing while he was gone.
preview.id: Lin Yue memulai perjalanan naik kembali ke Langit, menyadari kesaksian fana telah mengubah dirinya—dan mendapati Langit pun berubah saat ia pergi.

content.en:
Lin Yue left the river valley before dawn. The town still slept, and the temple bell was quiet, as if resting after calling everyone except the one person who most deserved relief.

He walked with the Emperor’s sealed decree pressed against his ribs and with three mortal testimonies pressed against his conscience. The papers in his satchel did not glow. They did not command. They only weighed.

He expected the return to be easier. Downward travel had felt like falling. Upward travel, he assumed, would feel like being lifted.

Instead, it felt like climbing.

The invisible seal around his ankles reminded him of every constraint he had obeyed. It was not punishment. It was accounting.

At the mountain pass where he had first arrived, Lin Yue found the air thinning in a way that had nothing to do with altitude. The mist began to behave again—less like weather, more like filing smoke into shelves.

A narrow path of cloud appeared, precise as a line on a ledger.

Lin Yue stepped onto it.

The world below dulled, and the world above sharpened. He felt his mortal disguise loosen like a borrowed coat.

But before he could take comfort, he heard voices.

Not mortal voices.

Administrative voices.

Two inspector spirits waited on the cloud path ahead, faceless and immaculate. Their robes carried no constellations, only stamps.

Lin Yue stopped.

One inspector asked for his return receipt.

Lin Yue blinked. He had expected a greeting, or at least silence. A receipt was a detail so small it felt like mockery.

He reached into his sleeve and produced the courier token the gatekeeper had marked into his shadow. The inspectors examined it as if inspecting meat.

The second inspector asked if the decree had been opened.

Lin Yue said no.

The inspectors nodded, almost satisfied.

Then the first inspector asked for the testimonies.

Lin Yue hesitated.

He realized he had not prepared for this moment. He had imagined delivering testimony directly to the minister of decrees or to the Jade Emperor, where words could be held in context.

Here, on a cloud path, context did not exist. There were only categories.

Lin Yue offered the bundle.

The inspectors did not read it. They weighed it—literally, in their hands, like assessing whether truth had the correct mass.

‘These are unsealed,’ one said, disapproving.

Lin Yue replied carefully that mortal testimony could not be sealed without breaking constraints.

The inspector’s head tilted, as if the concept of constraints applying to Heaven was offensive.

‘Mortal words are unreliable,’ the inspector said. ‘They must be standardized.’

Standardized.

Lin Yue felt the monk’s warning about purity and Wei Lan’s warning about fate collide into a new dread.

Standardization was how truth became safe.

Safe for whom?

The inspectors instructed Lin Yue to summarize.

He said he could not. The mission required exact recording.

The first inspector’s posture stiffened. ‘Exact recording produces noise. Heaven does not govern noise.’

Lin Yue’s chest tightened. He understood suddenly that this was the real battlefield: not between Heaven and mortals, but between testimony and administration.

He tried to explain that the three witnesses did not agree, and that their disagreement was itself evidence.

The second inspector responded with a phrase that sounded like doctrine: ‘Disagreement is impurity.’

Lin Yue heard, in that sentence, the seed of a catastrophe. If Heaven treated contradiction as dirt, Heaven would only accept lies—the kind that neatly matched.

Lin Yue did something risky.

He did not reveal miracles.

He did not threaten.

He did not bribe.

He simply refused to summarize.

He said, with a steadiness he did not know he possessed: ‘If Heaven needs clean answers, Heaven does not want answers. Heaven wants permission to stay the same.’

Silence.

The inspectors did not move. They did not react with anger. They reacted the way bureaucracy reacts to an unscheduled truth: with delay.

They said Lin Yue must submit his materials to a review office before proceeding.

Lin Yue asked how long.

They answered: ‘As long as necessary.’

Lin Yue realized delay could be a form of censorship that required no violence.

He looked down the cloud path. Behind him was the mortal world, messy and loud. Ahead was Heaven, clean and quiet.

He felt the temptation to comply. Compliance was a familiar warmth.

But then he thought of Judge Ren’s sentence: order is easy; justice costs.

He thought of the monk’s confession: nobody is clean; if Heaven only listens to purity, Heaven will only hear liars.

He thought of Wei Lan’s refusal to bow: calling choices ‘fate’ is a trick.

Lin Yue inhaled and made a decision that would have been unthinkable in Chapter 1.

He asked the inspectors to grant him direct audience with the minister of decrees.

They refused.

Lin Yue asked for the legal basis.

The inspectors paused.

That pause was all Lin Yue needed. A pause was a crack—small, but real.

He stepped past them.

The cloud path shuddered. The invisible seal around his ankles tightened so sharply it felt like pain for the first time. Not punishment—warning.

The inspectors did not chase him. They recorded.

Lin Yue understood: once something is recorded in Heaven, it becomes real, even if it is wrong.

He continued forward anyway.

As he neared the upward gate, the air became ink-scented again—the same omen as Chapter 1. But now Lin Yue recognized it not as fate.

It was activity.

Heaven was rewriting itself.

The gate opened into a corridor that looked familiar, yet off by a fraction, like a copied document with a subtle error.

Lanterns were lit too early. Clerks moved too quickly. Stamps hit paper with the rhythm of panic.

Lin Yue saw something he had never seen in Heaven: a line.

Not a procession. Not a ceremony.

A queue.

Spirits waited outside an office marked REVIEW OF THE MANDATE. Their faces were blank with fear.

Lin Yue walked past them toward the Hall of Radiant Administration, but a junior minister intercepted him.

The minister’s smile was perfect and exhausted. He said Lin Yue’s return had been anticipated.

He asked for the testimonies.

Lin Yue tightened his grip on the satchel.

The minister said gently, ‘We will help you. We will cleanse the noise. The Emperor needs a clear report.’

Lin Yue heard the same word again.

Cleanse.

He realized the investigation had already developed a preference: it preferred answers that could be filed.

Lin Yue bowed and said, ‘Then Heaven will receive what mortals gave: unclean truth.’

The minister’s smile cracked.

For a moment, Lin Yue saw fear beneath the polish. Not fear of the Emperor.

Fear of what the Emperor might actually do if he heard.

Lin Yue was escorted, not to the Emperor, but to a holding room—a place for documents that arrived at inconvenient times.

The door closed.

Lin Yue sat alone with his satchel, listening to the sounds of Heaven working faster than it should.

He realized Chapter 5’s turning point: the mission was never only about delivering testimony.

It was about whether Heaven could tolerate being contradicted.

Outside his door, clerks stamped and filed. Somewhere, someone was deciding what truth was allowed to look like.

Lin Yue opened his notebook and began to make a second copy in his own hand.

Not to change the words.

To prevent them from being cleaned.

Chapter 5 ends with Lin Yue writing in a locked room in Heaven, understanding that the most dangerous journey is not down into the mortal world.

It is up into the place that believes it is already right.
content.id:
Lin Yue meninggalkan lembah sungai sebelum fajar. Kota masih tidur, dan lonceng kuil sunyi, seakan beristirahat setelah memanggil semua orang kecuali satu orang yang paling layak mendapat lega.

Ia berjalan dengan maklumat Kaisar tersegel menekan rusuk dan tiga kesaksian fana menekan nurani. Kertas-kertas di tasnya tidak bersinar. Tidak memerintah. Hanya memberat.

Ia mengira perjalanan pulang akan lebih mudah. Perjalanan turun terasa seperti jatuh. Perjalanan naik, pikirnya, akan terasa seperti diangkat.

Ternyata rasanya seperti memanjat.

Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kakinya mengingatkan setiap batasan yang ia patuhi. Itu bukan hukuman. Itu pembukuan.

Di celah pegunungan tempat ia pertama kali tiba, Lin Yue merasakan udara menipis dengan cara yang tidak terkait ketinggian. Kabut mulai berperilaku lagi—kurang seperti cuaca, lebih seperti asap yang dibereskan ke rak.

Sebuah jalur sempit dari awan muncul, presisi seperti garis di buku kas.

Lin Yue melangkah ke atasnya.

Dunia bawah memudar, dunia atas menajam. Ia merasakan penyamarannya sebagai manusia mengendur seperti mantel pinjaman.

Namun sebelum ia sempat lega, ia mendengar suara.

Bukan suara manusia.

Suara administrasi.

Dua roh inspektur menunggu di jalur awan, tanpa wajah dan tanpa noda. Jubah mereka tidak membawa rasi bintang, hanya cap.

Lin Yue berhenti.

Satu inspektur meminta tanda terima pulang.

Lin Yue tertegun. Ia mengira akan disambut, atau setidaknya dibiarkan. Tanda terima terasa terlalu kecil sampai seperti ejekan.

Ia mengeluarkan token kurir yang ditandai penjaga gerbang ke bayangannya. Para inspektur memeriksanya seperti memeriksa daging.

Inspektur kedua bertanya apakah maklumat dibuka.

Lin Yue berkata tidak.

Para inspektur mengangguk, nyaris puas.

Lalu inspektur pertama meminta kesaksian.

Lin Yue ragu.

Ia sadar ia belum menyiapkan momen ini. Ia membayangkan menyerahkan kesaksian langsung pada menteri maklumat atau Kaisar Giok, tempat kata-kata punya konteks.

Di jalur awan ini, konteks tidak ada. Yang ada hanya kategori.

Lin Yue menyerahkan bungkusan.

Para inspektur tidak membacanya. Mereka menimbangnya—secara literal—seperti menilai apakah kebenaran punya massa yang benar.

‘Ini tidak tersegel,’ kata salah satu, tidak suka.

Lin Yue menjawab hati-hati bahwa kesaksian fana tidak bisa disegel tanpa melanggar batasan.

Kepala inspektur miring, seakan gagasan bahwa batasan bisa berlaku pada Langit itu menghina.

‘Kata-kata fana tidak bisa diandalkan,’ kata inspektur. ‘Harus distandardisasi.’

Standardisasi.

Di dalam Lin Yue, peringatan biksu tentang kemurnian dan peringatan Wei Lan tentang takdir bertabrakan menjadi takut baru.

Standardisasi adalah cara membuat kebenaran aman.

Aman untuk siapa?

Para inspektur menyuruh Lin Yue merangkum.

Lin Yue berkata ia tidak bisa. Misi mewajibkan pencatatan persis.

Postur inspektur menegang. ‘Pencatatan persis menghasilkan bising. Langit tidak memerintah bising.’

Dada Lin Yue menegang. Ia paham mendadak ini medan perang sebenarnya: bukan Langit melawan manusia, melainkan kesaksian melawan administrasi.

Ia mencoba menjelaskan bahwa tiga saksi tidak sepakat, dan ketidaksepakatan itu sendiri adalah bukti.

Inspektur kedua menjawab dengan frasa yang terdengar seperti doktrin: ‘Ketidaksepakatan adalah ketidakmurnian.’

Lin Yue mendengar benih bencana di kalimat itu. Jika Langit menganggap kontradiksi sebagai kotoran, Langit hanya akan menerima bohong—bohong yang rapi dan cocok.

Lin Yue melakukan sesuatu yang berisiko.

Ia tidak memperlihatkan mukjizat.

Ia tidak mengancam.

Ia tidak menyuap.

Ia hanya menolak merangkum.

Ia berkata dengan keteguhan yang tidak ia tahu ia punya: ‘Kalau Langit butuh jawaban bersih, Langit bukan ingin jawaban. Langit ingin izin untuk tetap sama.’

Sunyi.

Para inspektur tidak marah. Mereka bereaksi seperti birokrasi bereaksi pada kebenaran tak terjadwal: menunda.

Mereka berkata Lin Yue harus menyerahkan materi ke kantor peninjauan sebelum boleh lanjut.

Lin Yue bertanya berapa lama.

Mereka menjawab: ‘Selama diperlukan.’

Lin Yue sadar penundaan bisa menjadi sensor tanpa kekerasan.

Ia menatap jalur awan. Di belakangnya dunia fana yang berantakan dan bising. Di depannya Langit yang bersih dan sunyi.

Ia tergoda patuh. Patuh adalah hangat yang familiar.

Namun ia teringat kalimat Hakim Ren: tertib itu mudah; adil itu berbiaya.

Ia teringat pengakuan biksu: tak ada yang bersih; kalau Langit hanya mendengar kemurnian, Langit hanya mendengar pembohong.

Ia teringat Wei Lan: menyebut pilihan ‘takdir’ adalah trik.

Lin Yue menarik napas dan membuat keputusan yang mustahil bagi dirinya di Chapter 1.

Ia meminta para inspektur memberi akses langsung ke menteri maklumat.

Mereka menolak.

Lin Yue meminta dasar hukumnya.

Para inspektur diam sejenak.

Diam itu cukup. Diam adalah retak—kecil, tapi nyata.

Lin Yue melangkah melewati mereka.

Jalur awan berguncang. Segel tak terlihat di pergelangan kakinya mengencang sampai terasa sakit untuk pertama kalinya. Bukan hukuman—peringatan.

Para inspektur tidak mengejar. Mereka mencatat.

Lin Yue mengerti: sekali sesuatu tercatat di Langit, ia menjadi nyata, meski ia salah.

Lin Yue tetap maju.

Saat mendekati gerbang naik, udara kembali berbau tinta—pertanda yang sama seperti Chapter 1. Namun kini Lin Yue mengenalinya bukan sebagai takdir.

Itu aktivitas.

Langit sedang menulis ulang dirinya.

Gerbang membuka ke koridor yang terasa familiar, namun meleset sedikit, seperti dokumen salinan dengan kesalahan halus.

Lentera dinyalakan terlalu dini. Para juru tulis bergerak terlalu cepat. Cap menghantam kertas dengan ritme panik.

Lin Yue melihat hal yang belum pernah ia lihat di Langit: antrean.

Bukan prosesi. Bukan seremoni.

Antrean.

Roh-roh menunggu di luar kantor bertuliskan PENINJAUAN MANDAT. Wajah mereka kosong oleh takut.

Lin Yue berjalan menuju Aula Administrasi Bercahaya, tetapi seorang menteri muda mencegat.

Senyum menteri itu sempurna sekaligus lelah. Ia berkata kepulangan Lin Yue sudah diantisipasi.

Ia meminta kesaksian.

Lin Yue mengeratkan genggaman pada tas.

Menteri itu berkata lembut, ‘Kami akan membantumu. Kami akan membersihkan bisingnya. Kaisar butuh laporan jelas.’

Lin Yue mendengar kata itu lagi.

Bersihkan.

Ia sadar penyelidikan sudah punya selera: selera pada jawaban yang bisa diberkas.

Lin Yue membungkuk dan berkata, ‘Kalau begitu Langit akan menerima apa yang manusia beri: kebenaran yang tidak bersih.’

Senyum menteri itu retak.

Sesaat Lin Yue melihat takut di bawah polesan. Bukan takut pada Kaisar.

Takut pada apa yang mungkin Kaisar lakukan kalau ia mendengar.

Lin Yue diantar, bukan ke Kaisar, melainkan ke ruang tunggu—tempat bagi dokumen yang datang di waktu yang tidak nyaman.

Pintu menutup.

Lin Yue duduk sendiri bersama tasnya, mendengar suara Langit bekerja lebih cepat dari seharusnya.

Ia sadar titik balik Chapter 5: misi ini tidak pernah hanya soal menyampaikan kesaksian.

Misi ini soal apakah Langit sanggup ditentang.

Di luar pintu, cap menghantam dan berkas bergerak. Di suatu tempat, seseorang sedang memutuskan kebenaran harus terlihat seperti apa.

Lin Yue membuka buku catatannya dan mulai membuat salinan kedua dengan tangannya sendiri.

Bukan untuk mengubah kata-kata.

Untuk mencegahnya 'dibersihkan'.

Chapter 5 berakhir saat Lin Yue menulis di ruangan terkunci di Langit, mengerti bahwa perjalanan paling berbahaya bukan turun ke dunia fana.

Perjalanan paling berbahaya adalah naik ke tempat yang yakin dirinya sudah benar.

content.en:
Lin Yue is placed in the holding room with his satchel and told to wait. The door shuts with the quiet certainty of a stamp.

Hours pass without footsteps.

Then a slot opens in the door. A hand slides in a stack of forms—approval requests, indexing sheets, classification grids.

A note sits on top: 'For your convenience. Please translate all testimonies into categories.'

Lin Yue reads the categories.

They are not neutral.

'Complaint against officials.' 'Complaint against temples.' 'Complaint against fate.' 'Complaint against self.'

No category exists for: 'All of the above at once.'

Lin Yue refuses.

The slot opens again. Another note: 'Uncategorized truth cannot be processed. Unprocessed truth cannot reach the Throne.'

Lin Yue understands the trap: Heaven will not silence him. Heaven will drown him in procedure.

So he does something that feels like sin in a bureaucracy.

He writes the testimonies again—but in the margins.

He copies Judge Ren’s sentences around the edges of the forms, weaving truth into paperwork so it cannot be separated without destroying the document.

He copies the monk’s confession into the blank spaces between boxes.

He writes Wei Lan’s harsh clarity across the bottom where signatures should go.

When the review office finally opens the door to collect the documents, a junior clerk frowns.

'These are corrupted,' the clerk says.

Lin Yue answers, 'No. They are accurate.'

The clerk stamps REJECTED.

But the stamp lands on Wei Lan’s line about fate being a shield, and the ink bleeds through the clerk’s stamp.

For a brief moment the clerk’s face changes—confusion, then recognition.

The clerk has read something without permission.

He quickly gathers the papers and leaves.

Lin Yue waits.

Late that night, the door opens again. A different clerk enters, older, hands trembling.

He whispers, 'Stop writing in the margins. People are starting to notice.'

Lin Yue asks, 'Is that a threat?'

The clerk shakes his head. 'It’s a warning. The machine is frightened. And frightened machines become cruel.'

Chapter 5 ends with Lin Yue realizing that in Heaven, censorship is not only deletion.

It is formatting.

And the first resistance is not shouting.

It is refusing to let truth be made tidy.
content.id:
Lin Yue ditempatkan di ruang tunggu bersama tasnya dan disuruh menunggu. Pintu menutup dengan kepastian sunyi seperti cap.

Berjam-jam berlalu tanpa langkah.

Lalu sebuah celah di pintu terbuka. Sebuah tangan menyelipkan setumpuk formulir—permohonan persetujuan, lembar indeks, kisi-kisi klasifikasi.

Ada catatan di paling atas: 'Untuk memudahkan. Mohon terjemahkan semua kesaksian ke dalam kategori.'

Lin Yue membaca kategorinya.

Kategori itu tidak netral.

'Keluhan pada pejabat.' 'Keluhan pada kuil.' 'Keluhan pada takdir.' 'Keluhan pada diri.'

Tidak ada kategori untuk: 'Semuanya sekaligus.'

Lin Yue menolak.

Celah terbuka lagi. Catatan lain: 'Kebenaran tanpa kategori tidak dapat diproses. Kebenaran yang tidak diproses tidak bisa mencapai Tahta.'

Lin Yue paham jebakannya: Langit tidak akan membungkamnya. Langit akan menenggelamkannya dalam prosedur.

Maka ia melakukan sesuatu yang terasa seperti dosa dalam birokrasi.

Ia menulis ulang kesaksian—di pinggir.

Ia menyalin kalimat-kalimat Hakim Ren mengitari tepi formulir, menenun kebenaran ke dalam berkas agar tidak bisa dipisahkan tanpa merusak dokumen.

Ia menyalin pengakuan biksu ke ruang kosong di antara kotak-kotak.

Ia menulis kejernihan Wei Lan di bagian bawah tempat tanda tangan seharusnya.

Ketika kantor peninjauan akhirnya membuka pintu untuk mengambil dokumen, seorang juru tulis muda mengernyit.

'Ini rusak,' katanya.

Lin Yue menjawab, 'Tidak. Ini akurat.'

Juru tulis itu mengecap DITOLAK.

Namun cap itu jatuh tepat di kalimat Wei Lan tentang takdir sebagai tameng, dan tinta kebenaran menembus cap.

Sesaat wajah juru tulis itu berubah—bingung, lalu paham.

Ia membaca sesuatu tanpa izin.

Ia cepat-cepat mengumpulkan berkas dan pergi.

Lin Yue menunggu.

Larut malam, pintu terbuka lagi. Juru tulis berbeda masuk, lebih tua, tangannya gemetar.

Ia berbisik, 'Jangan menulis di pinggir. Orang mulai sadar.'

Lin Yue bertanya, 'Itu ancaman?'

Juru tulis itu menggeleng. 'Itu peringatan. Mesin ini takut. Dan mesin yang takut akan jadi kejam.'

Chapter 5 berakhir ketika Lin Yue sadar bahwa di Langit, sensor bukan hanya penghapusan.

Sensor adalah pemformatan.

Dan perlawanan pertama bukan teriak.

Perlawanan pertama adalah menolak membuat kebenaran rapi.

content.en:
On the cloud path, the inspectors do not block Lin Yue with force. They block him with courtesy.

They tell him the testimonies are valuable. They tell him the Emperor will be grateful.

Then they offer him a new title.

A promotion.

They say that delivering raw testimony is risky and thankless work, but translating it into a clean report is the kind of skill Heaven rewards.

They promise him a desk closer to the Hall of Radiant Administration. Better ink. Better quarters. A name that will be spoken with respect.

Lin Yue feels the temptation as warmth. Not greed—belonging.

The inspectors make it sound like duty: 'You are a scribe. Let scribes do what scribes do.'

Lin Yue almost agrees. He can already picture the relief of being useful in a way Heaven understands.

Then he remembers Wei Lan: truth treats others as equals.

He realizes what the promotion really buys: permission to stop listening to mortals as equals.

It buys a ladder.

Lin Yue asks one question: 'If I accept, who will read the raw words?'

The inspectors smile as if he is being naive. 'No one needs to. That is why we have you.'

Lin Yue declines.

The warmth vanishes.

The inspectors’ voices stay polite, but their politeness becomes steel. They mark his refusal and inform him that his materials must go through review.

Lin Yue steps forward anyway.

As the seal tightens on his ankles, he understands that Heaven’s most effective bribes are not pleasures.

They are roles.

Chapter 5 ends with Lin Yue choosing to remain a courier instead of becoming a translator of pain—and learning that the safest way to betray truth is to 'improve' it.
content.id:
Di jalur awan, para inspektur tidak menghalangi Lin Yue dengan kekerasan. Mereka menghalanginya dengan sopan.

Mereka berkata kesaksian itu berharga. Mereka berkata Kaisar akan berterima kasih.

Lalu mereka menawarkan gelar baru.

Kenaikan pangkat.

Mereka berkata mengantar kesaksian mentah itu berisiko dan tidak dihargai, tetapi menerjemahkannya menjadi laporan bersih adalah keterampilan yang dihargai Langit.

Mereka menjanjikan meja lebih dekat ke Aula Administrasi Bercahaya. Tinta lebih baik. Ruang tinggal lebih baik. Nama yang akan disebut dengan hormat.

Lin Yue merasakan godaan itu sebagai hangat. Bukan serakah—rasa memiliki.

Para inspektur membingkainya sebagai tugas: 'Kau juru tulis. Biarkan juru tulis melakukan tugasnya.'

Lin Yue nyaris setuju. Ia bisa membayangkan lega menjadi berguna dengan cara yang dimengerti Langit.

Lalu ia teringat Wei Lan: kebenaran memperlakukan orang lain sebagai setara.

Ia sadar kenaikan pangkat itu sebenarnya membeli apa: izin untuk berhenti mendengar manusia sebagai setara.

Itu membeli tangga.

Lin Yue bertanya satu hal: 'Kalau aku menerima, siapa yang akan membaca kata-kata mentahnya?'

Para inspektur tersenyum seakan Lin Yue naif. 'Tidak perlu. Itulah gunanya kau.'

Lin Yue menolak.

Hangat itu lenyap.

Suara para inspektur tetap sopan, tetapi sopannya menjadi baja. Mereka mencatat penolakannya dan berkata materi harus lewat peninjauan.

Lin Yue tetap melangkah.

Saat segel mengencang di pergelangan kakinya, Lin Yue mengerti suap paling efektif di Langit bukan kenikmatan.

Suap itu peran.

Chapter 5 berakhir ketika Lin Yue memilih tetap menjadi kurir alih-alih menjadi penerjemah derita—dan belajar bahwa cara paling aman mengkhianati kebenaran adalah dengan 'memperbaikinya'.

content.en:
Inside Heaven, Lin Yue expects opposition. What he does not expect is applause.

A junior minister greets him with rehearsed admiration. Clerks bow. Someone calls his mission 'historic.'

Lin Yue is escorted not to a holding room, but to a gallery.

A public gallery.

Below, a committee sits beneath banners reading REVIEW OF THE MANDATE. Seats are full. Scribes copy every gesture.

Lin Yue realizes the investigation has become performance.

The committee invites him to present.

They ask him to read the testimonies aloud.

Lin Yue begins with Judge Ren.

The committee nods solemnly, then immediately proposes a 'reform initiative'—a new stamp, a new audit schedule, a new slogan.

Lin Yue reads the monk.

They nod again and propose a 'purity clarification'—new guidelines for temples, new optics, new ceremonies.

Lin Yue reads Wei Lan.

The committee smiles sadly and declares it a 'regrettable misunderstanding of faith.'

Lin Yue feels sick.

They are turning each testimony into a solution that keeps the structure intact.

They thank him for his bravery.

They offer him a commemorative seal.

Lin Yue realizes he is being used as proof that Heaven listens.

Proof that costs Heaven nothing.

So he stops reading.

He holds up his notebook and says one sentence: 'If the review ends with new stamps, it is not a review. It is branding.'

The gallery goes silent.

A senior official rises and says Lin Yue is emotional, and emotions are a mortal contaminant.

Lin Yue replies, 'Then record this contamination. Because it is what your categories cannot hold.'

Clerks hesitate.

One clerk, trembling, writes the sentence anyway.

That small act ripples through the room: for the first time, the performance contains an unscripted line.

Chapter 5 ends with Lin Yue being removed politely from the gallery—and with one dangerous fact now recorded in an official hand: the possibility that accountability cannot be staged.
content.id:
Di dalam Langit, Lin Yue mengira akan ada perlawanan. Yang ia tidak duga justru tepuk tangan.

Seorang menteri muda menyambutnya dengan kagum yang terasa seperti latihan. Para juru tulis membungkuk. Seseorang menyebut misinya 'bersejarah.'

Lin Yue tidak diantar ke ruang tunggu, melainkan ke galeri.

Galeri publik.

Di bawah, sebuah komite duduk di bawah spanduk bertuliskan PENINJAUAN MANDAT. Kursi penuh. Para penyalin menulis setiap gestur.

Lin Yue sadar penyelidikan ini telah jadi pertunjukan.

Komite mempersilakan ia mempresentasikan.

Mereka meminta ia membacakan kesaksian keras-keras.

Lin Yue mulai dengan Hakim Ren.

Komite mengangguk khidmat, lalu segera mengusulkan 'inisiatif reformasi'—cap baru, jadwal audit baru, slogan baru.

Lin Yue membacakan biksu.

Mereka mengangguk lagi dan mengusulkan 'klarifikasi kemurnian'—panduan baru untuk kuil, optik baru, seremoni baru.

Lin Yue membacakan Wei Lan.

Komite tersenyum sedih dan menyebutnya 'kesalahpahaman yang menyedihkan tentang iman.'

Lin Yue mual.

Mereka mengubah setiap kesaksian menjadi solusi yang membuat struktur tetap utuh.

Mereka berterima kasih atas keberaniannya.

Mereka menawarkan segel peringatan.

Lin Yue sadar ia sedang dipakai sebagai bukti bahwa Langit mendengar.

Bukti yang tidak membuat Langit membayar apa pun.

Maka ia berhenti membaca.

Ia mengangkat buku catatannya dan berkata satu kalimat: 'Kalau peninjauan berakhir dengan cap baru, itu bukan peninjauan. Itu branding.'

Galeri sunyi.

Seorang pejabat senior berdiri dan berkata Lin Yue emosional, dan emosi adalah kontaminan fana.

Lin Yue menjawab, 'Kalau begitu catat kontaminasi ini. Karena inilah yang tidak bisa ditampung kategori kalian.'

Para penyalin ragu.

Satu penyalin, gemetar, menulis kalimat itu juga.

Tindakan kecil itu merambat di ruangan: untuk pertama kalinya, pertunjukan memuat satu kalimat di luar naskah.

Chapter 5 berakhir ketika Lin Yue dikeluarkan dengan sopan dari galeri—dan satu fakta berbahaya kini tercatat oleh tangan resmi: kemungkinan bahwa pertanggungjawaban tidak bisa dipentaskan.

content.en:
Lin Yue is writing his second copy in the holding room when a shadow appears beneath the door.

Not an inspector.

Not a clerk.

A person.

The door opens, and the disgraced monk steps inside, robe dusty, eyes calm as if Heaven is just another marketplace.

Lin Yue stands in shock. 'You can’t be here.'

The monk shrugs. 'They didn’t stop me. No one imagines a disgraced man belongs in Heaven. That’s why I walked through.'

Lin Yue feels the seal on his ankles tighten, not from violation but from impossibility.

The monk looks around the holding room and smiles bitterly. 'So this is where truth waits to be cleaned.'

Lin Yue demands an explanation.

The monk says the faceless statue’s road was not merely abandoned. It was a thin place. A seam between worlds where symbols wear out.

He says when a symbol wears out, even Heaven’s border loses confidence.

Disgrace, in other words, becomes a kind of invisibility.

The monk sits and begins speaking—not testimony, but strategy.

He tells Lin Yue that Heaven is not one will. It is a hierarchy of fears. Some fear the Emperor. Some fear chaos. Some fear losing the beautiful story.

He tells Lin Yue to choose his target carefully.

'Don’t fight the whole machine,' the monk says. 'Find the clerk who still remembers hunger.'

Lin Yue asks why the monk helps.

The monk answers, 'Because I said nobody is clean. That includes Heaven. I came to make sure your report doesn’t pretend otherwise.'

Before Lin Yue can respond, footsteps approach.

The monk stands and places a finger to his lips. He slips behind a screen, vanishing into the room’s shadows like a stain everyone refuses to acknowledge.

A clerk enters and sees only Lin Yue and paperwork.

Lin Yue realizes the monk’s greatest weapon is the one the world used against him: being ignored.

Chapter 5 ends with Lin Yue holding his pen above the page, aware that he is no longer alone—and that Heaven has just been infiltrated by the one kind of person it never audits: someone it has already decided doesn’t count.
content.id:
Lin Yue sedang menulis salinan kedua di ruang tunggu ketika sebuah bayangan muncul di bawah pintu.

Bukan inspektur.

Bukan juru tulis.

Seorang manusia.

Pintu terbuka, dan biksu yang dipermalukan melangkah masuk, jubahnya berdebu, matanya tenang seolah Langit hanya pasar lain.

Lin Yue berdiri terkejut. 'Kau tidak mungkin ada di sini.'

Biksu itu mengangkat bahu. 'Mereka tidak menghentikanku. Tak ada yang membayangkan orang aib punya tempat di Langit. Itu sebabnya aku bisa lewat.'

Lin Yue merasakan segel di pergelangan kaki mengencang, bukan karena pelanggaran, tetapi karena mustahil.

Biksu itu menatap ruangan tunggu dan tersenyum getir. 'Jadi ini tempat kebenaran menunggu untuk dibersihkan.'

Lin Yue menuntut penjelasan.

Biksu itu berkata jalan patung tanpa wajah itu bukan sekadar ditinggalkan. Itu tempat tipis. Jahitan antar dunia, tempat simbol-simbol aus.

Ia berkata ketika simbol aus, bahkan perbatasan Langit kehilangan percaya diri.

Aib, dengan kata lain, menjadi sejenis ketak-terlihatan.

Biksu itu duduk dan mulai bicara—bukan kesaksian, melainkan strategi.

Ia berkata Langit bukan satu kehendak. Langit hierarki ketakutan. Ada yang takut Kaisar. Ada yang takut chaos. Ada yang takut kehilangan cerita indah.

Ia menyuruh Lin Yue memilih sasaran dengan hati-hati.

'Jangan melawan seluruh mesin,' katanya. 'Temukan juru tulis yang masih ingat lapar.'

Lin Yue bertanya kenapa ia membantu.

Biksu itu menjawab, 'Karena aku bilang tak ada yang bersih. Itu termasuk Langit. Aku datang memastikan laporanmu tidak pura-pura sebaliknya.'

Sebelum Lin Yue sempat menjawab, langkah kaki mendekat.

Biksu itu berdiri dan menempelkan jari ke bibir. Ia menyelinap di balik penyekat, lenyap ke bayangan ruangan seperti noda yang semua orang menolak akui.

Seorang juru tulis masuk dan hanya melihat Lin Yue dan berkas.

Lin Yue sadar senjata terbesar biksu itu adalah yang dunia pakai untuk menghancurkannya: diabaikan.

Chapter 5 berakhir ketika Lin Yue menahan pena di atas halaman, sadar ia tidak lagi sendirian—dan Langit baru saja disusupi oleh satu jenis orang yang tidak pernah diaudit: orang yang sudah diputuskan sejak awal tidak dihitung.

preview.en: Lin Yue fights Heaven’s slow censorship, finds an unexpected ally inside the clerks, and forces the testimonies one step closer to the Jade Emperor.
preview.id: Lin Yue melawan sensor halus Langit, menemukan sekutu tak terduga di kalangan juru tulis, dan mendorong kesaksian selangkah lebih dekat ke Kaisar Giok.

title.en: The Clerk Who Still Remembers Hunger
title.id: Juru Tulis yang Masih Ingat Lapar

content.en:
The holding room in Heaven had no window, because windows implied weather, and weather implied unpredictability. This room was for inconvenient things that needed to exist without being seen.

Lin Yue wrote.

He made a second copy of every testimony with a hand that had become steadier since the mortal world. Not because he felt brave, but because he had learned that steadiness was sometimes the only form of mercy bureaucracy understood.

Outside, stamps fell like rain. Clerks moved like ants carrying law.

At the third hour of waiting, the door opened.

A junior clerk entered carrying a tray: water, ink, and a small stack of review forms. The clerk’s face was young enough to still show fear when he tried to hide it.

He bowed and said, politely, that the review office required Lin Yue to submit an abstract.

Lin Yue did not look up from his page.

He said, without hostility, 'An abstract is a summary. Summaries are where truth goes to become useful.'

The clerk’s hands tightened around the tray. He whispered that refusal would delay processing.

Lin Yue’s pen paused. He asked, 'Is delay your job, or your habit?'

The clerk flinched.

Then, very quietly, the clerk answered, 'Both.'

Lin Yue set down his pen and studied the clerk’s sleeves. They were clean, as expected. But the cuff stitching was repaired by hand—uneven, careful, like mortal mending.

Lin Yue asked where the clerk learned that stitch.

The clerk hesitated, then said he had been mortal once, in a village that lost a harvest to a late frost. His mother repaired clothes by candlelight because cloth was more expensive than pride.

Lin Yue felt something shift. Heaven was not made only of gods. It was made of former humans too—humans who had been promoted into eternity without being fully released from memory.

Lin Yue asked the clerk a direct question: 'Do you think the Mandate can be wrong?'

The clerk’s eyes widened. That question was dangerous even in whisper.

He did not answer. Instead he placed the review forms on the desk and said, 'I am required to tell you which boxes to tick.'

Lin Yue slid one page toward him—Judge Ren’s testimony—without permission.

The clerk recoiled as if the ink were poison.

Lin Yue did not push it closer. He only said, 'Read one sentence. If it is noise, you can report me.'

The clerk looked at the closed door, at the empty corners, at the ceiling where no one watched except rules.

Then he read.

Judge Ren’s line about order and justice entered the room like a blade.

The clerk’s face changed. It was subtle—like a person remembering hunger in the middle of a feast.

He whispered, 'That sentence should not exist in a box.'

Lin Yue nodded. 'Exactly.'

The clerk swallowed and asked why Lin Yue would risk punishment.

Lin Yue answered: 'Because delay is also harm. It just wears clean sleeves.'

The clerk looked down at the review forms again, then did something small and revolutionary.

He flipped the forms over.

On the blank back, he began copying lines from the testimonies in a cramped, fast hand.

Lin Yue watched, stunned.

The clerk said, 'If I submit your words, they will cleanse them. If I submit their words in my handwriting, they will have to cleanse me too.'

Lin Yue understood the strategy. In Heaven, documents could be purified. People were harder.

The clerk continued copying.

He copied the monk’s line about cleanliness.

He copied Wei Lan’s line about fate as a shield.

Each sentence became a stain on official paper.

When he finished, he sealed the stack with a clerk’s stamp. The stamp was not an authorization. It was a complication.

He stood, hands trembling now.

Lin Yue asked his name.

The clerk hesitated, then said, 'Shen.'

Lin Yue repeated it carefully. A name was not a title. A name was a person.

Shen bowed and moved to the door.

Before leaving, he said, 'They will summon you to the review chamber soon. Do not speak in their language. If you speak in their language, they will win.'

Lin Yue asked what language he should speak.

Shen replied, almost inaudible, 'Speak as if the Emperor is listening already.'

The door closed.

Minutes later, two guards arrived—not violent, but precise. They escorted Lin Yue through corridors that had become busier since his departure, through offices where clerks stamped faster than thought.

He passed the queue again. Spirits waited with expressions that tried to be patient and failed.

At last he was brought into the review chamber.

The chamber was bright, with high desks and mirrored floors designed to make everyone feel visible. A panel of officials sat behind a screen of silk, as if they needed fabric to protect them from what they judged.

A voice asked Lin Yue to present his summary.

Lin Yue held up the sealed imperial scroll in one hand and Shen’s stamped pages in the other.

He said, calmly, 'The summary is this: mortals say the Mandate has become a mask. If Heaven files that sentence away, Heaven will be filing away itself.'

Silence stretched.

A second voice accused Lin Yue of emotional contamination.

Lin Yue replied, 'If you call pain contamination, you are admitting you cannot govern reality.'

A low murmur rose behind the silk.

Lin Yue did not raise his voice. He did not argue like a lawyer.

He spoke like a courier delivering words intact.

He recited three sentences—one from each witness—without commentary.

The room tightened as if the sentences had weight.

Then a new sound entered: footsteps approaching with no haste.

The officials behind the silk stopped murmuring.

The door at the far end opened.

A senior minister entered carrying a thin tablet of jade.

He announced that the Jade Emperor would not grant full audience yet.

But the Emperor had ordered that the raw testimonies be preserved—unaltered—in the Archive of First Drafts.

Lin Yue caught the irony. Heaven had created an archive for truth it did not want to use.

Still, 'preserved unaltered' was a victory measured in millimeters.

Lin Yue bowed and accepted the ruling.

As he was escorted back out, Lin Yue saw Shen briefly at a side desk, face blank again, as if nothing had happened.

But Shen’s fingers were stained with ink.

Lin Yue realized Chapter 6’s lesson: in a bureaucracy, revolutions rarely look like shouting crowds.

They look like one clerk deciding to copy a sentence that is inconvenient.

Chapter 6 ends with Lin Yue walking toward the Archive of First Drafts, knowing the testimonies were not yet heard by the Emperor—but they were now harder to erase.

And Heaven, for the first time, had to acknowledge that unclean truth existed inside its own walls.
content.id:
Ruang tunggu di Langit tidak punya jendela, karena jendela menyiratkan cuaca, dan cuaca menyiratkan ketakterdugaan. Ruang ini dibuat untuk hal-hal yang tidak nyaman: ada, tetapi tak terlihat.

Lin Yue menulis.

Ia membuat salinan kedua dari setiap kesaksian dengan tangan yang kini lebih stabil sejak dunia fana. Bukan karena ia berani, melainkan karena ia belajar bahwa kestabilan kadang satu-satunya bentuk belas kasih yang dimengerti birokrasi.

Di luar, cap jatuh seperti hujan. Para juru tulis bergerak seperti semut membawa hukum.

Pada jam ketiga menunggu, pintu terbuka.

Seorang juru tulis muda masuk membawa baki: air, tinta, dan setumpuk formulir peninjauan. Wajahnya cukup muda sampai rasa takut masih tampak ketika ia mencoba menyembunyikannya.

Ia membungkuk dan berkata, sopan, bahwa kantor peninjauan meminta Lin Yue menyerahkan abstrak.

Lin Yue tidak menoleh dari halaman.

Ia berkata tanpa bermusuhan, 'Abstrak adalah ringkasan. Ringkasan adalah tempat kebenaran pergi untuk menjadi berguna.'

Tangan juru tulis itu mengencang pada baki. Ia berbisik bahwa penolakan akan menunda pemrosesan.

Pena Lin Yue berhenti. Ia bertanya, 'Menunda itu tugasmu, atau kebiasaanmu?'

Juru tulis itu tersentak.

Lalu pelan sekali, ia menjawab, 'Dua-duanya.'

Lin Yue meletakkan pena dan memperhatikan ujung lengan baju juru tulis. Bersih, tentu. Tetapi jahitan mansetnya diperbaiki tangan—tidak rata, teliti, seperti tambalan fana.

Lin Yue bertanya di mana ia belajar jahitan itu.

Juru tulis ragu, lalu berkata ia dulu manusia, di desa yang gagal panen karena embun beku terlambat. Ibunya menambal baju di bawah cahaya lilin karena kain lebih mahal daripada harga diri.

Lin Yue merasakan sesuatu bergeser. Langit tidak hanya terdiri dari dewa. Langit juga terdiri dari mantan manusia—manusia yang dipromosikan ke keabadian tanpa benar-benar dilepas dari ingatan.
